alignments.preprint_claim_text: Epistatic interactions between residues modulate the mutation patterns involved in drug resistance in HIV. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Epistatic interactions between residues modulate the mutation patterns involved in drug resistance in HIV. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result statement verbatim, indicating no substantive change in meaning or strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evidence for long-range epistasis is strong as shown by the analysis of high-order marginals of the MSA distribution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The comparison of the Potts model predicted and experimental HIV sequence prevalences reveals unambiguous signatures of epistasis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert strong evidence for long-range epistasis, but the publication frames it specifically via Potts model vs experimental prevalence comparisons (more specific and emphatic).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Double mutant cycles reveal that the most strongly interacting mutations in HIV involve correlated sets of drug-resistance-associated residues. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental measures of fitness generally provide weak evidence for epistasis in viral systems due to probing fitness effects of point mutations in a single background. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports strong interactions from double mutant cycles, whereas the publication downplays experimental evidence for epistasis and highlights limitations of common experimental designs, effectively tempering the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis and comparison between model and experiment in double mutant cycles is complicated by the small dynamic range of measurements. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The detectable effects of epistasis are obscured by higher evolutionary conservation at sites. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Both are limitations affecting detectability of epistasis; the publication emphasizes site conservation as the obscuring factor whereas the preprint emphasized measurement dynamic range—both limit detectability but the publication reframes the cause.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Correlated models for the design of experiments can help identify the epistatic interactions involved in mutational escape and lead to better inhibitor therapies. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Global pairwise interaction Potts models are necessary for predicting the mutational landscape of viral proteins. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes a stronger, more general claim about the necessity of Potts (pairwise) models for predicting mutational landscapes, broadening and strengthening the preprint's proposal that correlated models aid experimental design and therapy development.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The stacked model integrating all modalities via stacking Elastic Net provided the best prediction (r = .57) and excellent test-retest reliability (ICC= ~ .85) in capturing general cognitive abilities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The stacked model integrating all modalities via stacking Elastic Net provided the best prediction (r = 0.57) and excellent test-retest reliability (ICC=∼.85) in capturing general cognitive abilities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative result and reliability metrics without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The stacked model integrating tfMRI across tasks led to significantly higher prediction (r = 0.56) compared to the stacked model integrating across non-task modalities (r = .27). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The stacked model integrating tfMRI across tasks led to significantly higher prediction (r = 0.56) compared to the stacked model integrating across non-task modalities (r = 0.27). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and metrics in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study suggests that tfMRI, when used appropriately, provides predictive and reliable sources of information for individual differences in cognitive abilities, more so than non-task modalities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study suggests that tfMRI, when used appropriately, provides predictive and reliable sources of information for individual differences in cognitive abilities, more so than non-task modalities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same interpretive conclusion retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results are consistent with the parieto-frontal integration theory of intelligence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results are consistent with the parieto-frontal integration theory of intelligence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the theoretical consistency claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach shows the importance of the fronto-parietal areas in cognition. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit contribution statement about fronto-parietal importance present in the preprint; the claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MechSpy generates hypotheses of the most likely mechanisms of toxicity by combining a semantically-interconnected knowledge representation with gene expression time series on human tissue. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MechSpy generates hypotheses of the most likely mechanisms of toxicity by combining a semantically-interconnected knowledge representation of human biology, toxicology, and biochemistry with gene expression time series on human tissue. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same capability while specifying the domains included in the knowledge representation, thereby clarifying the original description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MechSpy predicts the canonical mechanism of toxicity for many well-studied compounds and experimentally validates some predictions for other chemicals without an established mechanism of toxicity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MechSpy predicts the canonical mechanism of toxicity for many well-studied compounds and experimentally validates some predictions for other chemicals without an established mechanism of toxicity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the experimental prediction and validation claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework can be modified to include additional mechanisms of toxicity and is generalizable to other types of mechanisms of human biology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MechSpy can be modified to include additional mechanisms of toxicity and is generalizable to other types of mechanisms of human biology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim about extensibility and generalizability verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is currently no computational approach to generate mechanistic hypotheses to explain why chemicals elicit a toxic response. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MechSpy is an advantageous tool for predictive toxicology and is the first of its kind to produce a mechanistic explanation for each prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's gap statement (no existing approaches) is represented in the publication as MechSpy being the first such tool and advantageous, reframing the limitation as a claimed novelty/advantage.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Eye-of-origin information was decodable for the entire duration of stimulus presentation, but diminished in the deepest layers of V1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Eye-of-origin information was decodable for the entire duration of stimulus presentation, but diminished in the deepest layers of V1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical experimental result reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Orientation information was transient and equally pronounced across the microcircuit. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orientation information was transient and equally pronounced along all layers of V1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same finding, worded to specify 'layers of V1' consistent with the preprint's microcircuit phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When stimuli were repeated, information regarding orientation was enhanced at the expense of eye-of-origin information. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The primate primary visual cortex (V1) combines the two eyes' signals, extracts stimulus orientation, and integrates contextual information such as visual stimulation history. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a broader statement about V1 integrating stimulation history that encompasses the preprint's specific repetition-related tradeoff, but it does not restate the exact tradeoff, broadening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings provide empirical evidence that adjudicates between long-standing hypotheses regarding information transfer within the canonical cortical microcircuit. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using time-resolved MVPA, we were able to evaluate laminar response properties beyond those yielded by univariate analyses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the time-resolved MVPA methodological advance that enables evaluation of laminar responses, which underlies and clarifies the preprint's claim about adjudicating hypotheses.

alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate repeating (reverberating) patterns of neural activity that have not previously been observed using standard univariate approaches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit novel result about reverberating patterns not present as a distinct claim in the preprint; it extends the reported findings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spatial mapping of sites of metabolic perturbations revealed significantly lower metabolic perturbation in the trachea compared to other lung tissue sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatial mapping of sites of metabolic perturbations revealed significantly lower metabolic perturbation in the trachea compared to other lung tissue sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific spatial result is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using random forest machine learning, we identified metabolites that responded differently in each lung position based on infection, including specific amino acids, lipids and lipid-like molecules, and nucleosides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using random forest machine learning, we identified metabolites that responded differently in each lung position based on infection, including specific amino acids, lipids and lipid-like molecules, and nucleosides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same machine-learning identification result and metabolite categories.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings highlight the importance of a spatial approach to understanding the intersection between lung metabolome, viral infection and disease severity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings highlight the utility of a spatial approach to understanding the intersection between the lung metabolome, viral infection, and disease severity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses slightly different wording ('utility' vs 'importance') but conveys the same conclusion about the value of a spatial approach.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach will expand our understanding of respiratory disease pathogenesis and guide the development of novel host-directed therapeutics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the need for advances in IAV treatment, especially host-directed therapeutics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's forward-looking claim about expanding understanding and guiding therapeutic development is reframed in the publication as demonstrating a need for advances in treatment—a more cautious, narrower recommendation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PIVOT is the first machine learning model to classify genes as tumour suppressor genes, oncogenes, or neutral, thus assigning the functional impact of the gene in the patient. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PIVOT is the first machine learning model to classify genes as tumor suppressor genes, oncogenes, or neutral, thus assigning the functional impact of the gene in the patient. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the exact contribution claim with the same scope and emphasis; wording differences are minor (British vs. American spelling).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our models trained on multi-omic data improved predictions compared to mutation and expression data, achieving an accuracy ≥ 0.99 for BRCA, LUAD, and COAD datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models trained on multi-omic data improved predictions compared with mutation and expression data, achieving an accuracy ≥ 0.99 for BRCA, LUAD, and COAD datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative result and comparison with matching datasets and accuracy, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our predictions on BRCA, COAD, and LUAD cancer types reveal commonly altered genes such as TP53 and PIK3CA, which are predicted drivers for multiple cancer types. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PIVOT identifies both known driver genes and new driver genes such as PRKCA, SOX9, and PSMD4. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims address driver-gene identification but the publication emphasizes newly identified drivers (different gene examples) rather than the same commonly altered genes, indicating a shift in findings/ emphasis rather than a direct restatement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PIVOT labels personalised driver genes as TSGs and OGs and also identifies rare driver genes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PIVOT can label rare driver events occurring in as few as one sample. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the preprint's general claim about rare-driver detection by specifying sensitivity down to single-sample events, narrowing the previous general statement into a quantified capability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our multi-omic model labels both CNV and mutations with a more considerable contribution by CNV alterations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multi-omic model labels both CNV and mutations, with a more considerable contribution by CNV alterations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding about the relative contribution of CNVs versus mutations; wording and emphasis are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results reveal a two-state molecular conductance behavior for peptides across several different amino acid sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results reveal a two-state molecular conductance behavior for peptides across several different amino acid sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the primary experimental observation verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This two-state molecular conductance behavior arises due to the conformational flexibility of peptide backbones. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This two-state molecular conductance behavior arises due to the conformational flexibility of peptide backbones. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic attribution to conformational flexibility is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Secondary structure plays a key role in electron transport in peptides. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results show that secondary structure plays a key role in electron transport in peptides. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conclusion linking secondary structure and electron transport with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Molecular conformations from MD simulations are used to model charge transport in NEGF-DFT calculations, and the results are in reasonably good agreement with experiments. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include a claim about MD-derived conformations feeding NEGF-DFT calculations or explicit agreement with experiments, so this methodological/result claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work provides a basis for understanding energy flow in larger proteins or biomolecular assemblies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the broader, prospective claim about serving as a basis for understanding energy flow in larger systems, so this conclusion is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Technological and computational advances in structural biology allow for the resolution of biomolecular structures at the atomistic level using cryogenic electron microscopy and machine learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on the GlycoShape resource and concrete results rather than reiterating the general field-level claim, so this general technological claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Structural information has significantly supported the rapid development of COVID-19 vaccines based on the SARS-CoV-2 spike glycoprotein. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention COVID-19 vaccine development; the preprint's COVID-related example is absent from the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Glycosylation, the most abundant post-translational modification of proteins, is largely invisible through experimental structural biology and cannot be reproduced by machine learning due to a lack of data. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: While the publication addresses glycan coverage and algorithmic links, it does not restate this specific limitation claim about ML reproducibility and experimental invisibility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GlycoShape is an open access glycan structure database and toolbox designed to restore glycoproteins to their native functional form by supplementing structural information with missing glycans derived from molecular dynamics simulations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GlycoShape is an open-access glycan structure database and toolbox designed to restore glycoproteins to their native and functional form in seconds. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the core contribution (open-access glycan database/toolbox) and adds a speed claim ("in seconds"), which refines but does not contradict the preprint description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integration of GlycoShape with other open access protein structure databases can significantly enhance scientific discovery and pharmacological applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm Re-Glyco links glycan structures to proteins and is compatible with structural data in open-access repositories. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a concrete mechanism (Re-Glyco compatibility with repositories) that substantiates the preprint's general claim about integration enhancing discovery, clarifying how integration is enabled.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The greater sandhill crane's summering range in western North America is significantly shaped by landscape composition, particularly by riparian floodplains and wetlands. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wetland variables identified as important predictors of bird distributions were evaluated in a post hoc analysis to measure long-term effects of climate-driven surface water drying. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements link wetland-related landscape factors to bird distributions; the publication frames this as identification and post hoc evaluation of wetland predictors, which clarifies the original claim's analytical framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wetland drying from 1984 to 2022 represents an emerging ecological bottleneck that could limit the future summer range of greater sandhill cranes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wetland drying observed in portions of the range from 1984 to 2022 represented an emerging ecological bottleneck that could limit future greater sandhill crane summer range. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same temporal drying observation and its ecological implication with essentially identical wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Private lands account for 78% of predicted distributions of greater sandhill cranes, highlighting their critical role in the species' ecology. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Findings highlighted the role of private lands in greater sandhill crane ecology as they accounted for 78% of predicted distributions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same quantitative result about private lands' contribution to predicted distributions; scope and confidence are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides novel insight into the ecosystem services of flood-irrigated agriculture, which supports nearly 60% of the wetland resources used by greater sandhill cranes. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Study outcomes provide novel insight into the significance of ecosystem services provided by flood-irrigated agriculture that supported nearly 60% of wetland resources used by birds. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same finding about flood-irrigated agriculture supporting ~60% of wetland resources and frames it as novel insight, matching the preprint's contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater sandhill cranes function as an umbrella species for agroecology and climate change adaptation strategies aimed at reducing agricultural water use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater sandhill cranes function as a surrogate species for agroecology and climate change adaptation strategies seeking to reduce agricultural water use through improved efficiency. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses slightly different terminology ("surrogate" vs "umbrella") and emphasizes strategies for efficiency, which clarifies wording but preserves the overall conclusion and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CERA (ChatGPT-Enhanced ROC Analysis) tool is a user-friendly web tool designed for faster and more effective ROC curve analyses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CERA tool provides a user-friendly interface for ROC curve analysis, making it accessible to individuals without coding experience. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the core contribution: a user-friendly ROC analysis tool accessible to non-coders, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CERA enhances the accessibility and understanding of ROC analysis results by generating an interpreted report using R-Markdown. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CERA enhances the understanding of ROC analysis results by generating interpreted reports using R-Markdown. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim about R-Markdown interpreted reports improving understanding and accessibility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The necessity for data preprocessing and analysis in ROC curve analysis presents a significant challenge for individuals without coding experience. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The necessity for data preprocessing and analysis in ROC curve analysis presents a significant challenge for non-coders. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation about preprocessing and analysis barriers for people without coding skills.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed data-driven and generative modeling approach recapitulates the dynamical features of gait behaviors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A data-driven and generative modeling approach recapitulates the dynamical features of gait behaviors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The wording and claim are effectively identical between preprint and publication, conveying the same contribution and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gait signatures for healthy older adults and stroke survivors during treadmill walking are conserved across gait speed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gait signatures for healthy older adults and stroke survivors during treadmill walking are conserved across gait speed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same in substance and confidence in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Components of gait signatures can be biomechanically interpreted and manipulated to reveal their relationships to observed spatiotemporal joint coordination patterns. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Components of gait signatures can be biomechanically interpreted and manipulated to reveal their relationships to observed spatiotemporal joint coordination patterns. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Textual content is the same; only the label/type shifts from result to conclusion, not the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gait dynamics model can predict the time evolution of joint coordination based on an initial static posture. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The gait dynamics model can predict the time evolution of joint coordination based on an initial static posture. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and confidence in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gait signatures framework provides a generalizable, holistic method for characterizing and predicting cyclic, dynamical motor behavior. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No equivalent generalizable/holistic framework claim appears in the publication; the publication instead adds a different claim about individual differences.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Gait signatures reveal individual differences in gait dynamics, even in individuals with similar functional deficits. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim about individual differences is new relative to the preprint and has no clear preprint counterpart.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed models provide consistently good performance in comparison to existing alternatives for gene regulatory network reconstruction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed models provide consistently good performance in comparison to contenders whose performance varies with data sets from simulation and experiments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication refines "existing alternatives" to "contenders whose performance varies with data sets", clarifying the comparison context without changing the overall claim strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simultaneous modeling of several target genes results in improved inference of gene regulatory networks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simultaneous modelling of several target genes results in improved inference of gene regulatory networks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim is equivalent in content and confidence; differences are only orthographic.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identified master regulators are in line with experimental evidence from the model bacterium E. coli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The identified master regulators are in line with experimental evidence from the model bacterium E. coli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in content and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approaches blend regularized multivariate regression and graphical models to address gene regulatory network reconstruction. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models' formulation facilitates the prediction of master regulators across different environments. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The methodological description in the preprint is reframed in the publication to emphasize cross-environment prediction; this represents a substantive shift in emphasis rather than a verbatim match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated acoustic analyses of the voice can successfully detect sleep deprivation with accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art speech features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated acoustic analyses of the voice can successfully detect sleep deprivation with accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art speech features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim identical in substance and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sleep deprivation causes two distinct effects on the voice: a change in prosody and a change in timbre. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sleep deprivation causes distinct changes in the voice, including alterations in slow temporal modulations related to prosody and changes in spectral features related to voice quality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication expands and clarifies the acoustic descriptors (prosody -> slow temporal modulations; timbre -> spectral/voice quality) while retaining the two-effect structure.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relative balance of the effects of sleep deprivation on voice varies widely across individuals, indicating the need to characterize sleep deprivation at the individual level. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relative balance of the effects of sleep deprivation on voice varies widely across individuals, indicating the need for individual characterization of sleep deprivation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Substantively equivalent claim about inter-individual variability and the need for individual-level characterization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prosody factor correlates with subjective sleepiness reports, while the timbre factor does not, suggesting both explicit and implicit consequences of sleep deprivation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prosody factor of voice changes correlates with subjective sleepiness reports, while the voice quality factor does not, suggesting both explicit and implicit consequences of sleep deprivation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses slightly different terminology and elevates the framing to a conclusion, but the core finding and implication are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future investigations could correlate vocal markers of sleep deprivation with objective physiological measures, potentially leading to cost-effective 'sleep stethoscopes'. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future investigations could lead to the development of 'sleep stethoscopes' for cost-effective diagnosis of individual effects of sleep deprivation. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the future-translation motif but softens the certainty and frames it more narrowly as a diagnostic development, reducing stated confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cognitive inflexibility does not predispose rats to pathological weight loss in the activity-based anorexia model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cognitive inflexibility measured by the reversal learning task does not predispose rats to pathological weight loss in the activity-based anorexia model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the measurement detail (reversal learning task) but preserves the substantive null finding and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rats that are susceptible to weight loss performed better on the reversal learning task compared to those that are resistant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Rats predisposed to weight loss in the activity-based anorexia model were more quickly able to learn the reversal learning task prior to exposure to the model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the advantage was in learning speed prior to exposure; this clarifies timing and performance metric without changing directionality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ABA exposed (but weight recovered) rats performed much worse on the reversal learning task than ABA naïve rats. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ABA-exposed (but weight-recovered) rats performed much worse than ABA naïve rats on the reversal learning task. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is the same in content, but the publication reports a lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Animals trained on reversal learning were better able to resist weight loss upon subsequent exposure to the activity-based anorexia model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Animals trained on reversal learning were better able to resist weight loss upon subsequent exposure to the activity-based anorexia model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Substantively identical finding but reported with reduced certainty in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides a robust target for future studies investigating potential novel pharmacotherapies for anorexia nervosa. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study uncovered stable behavioral differences between ABA susceptible versus resistant rats that may highlight predictors of anorectic phenotypes. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the broader therapeutic-target claim into a more specific contribution about predictors and behavioral differences, narrowing the translational emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Changes to gut environmental factors such as pH and osmolality correlate with major shifts in microbiome composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Changes to gut environmental factors such as pH and osmolality correlate with major shifts in microbiome composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in content and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability to grow in extreme pH or osmolality conditions correlates with the availability of known stress response genes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ability to grow in extreme pH or osmolality conditions correlated with the availability of known stress response genes in many cases. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication hedges the generality by adding "in many cases," slightly tempering the claim's universality and thus the confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning analysis uncovered genes or subsystems that are predictive of differential tolerance in either acid or osmotic stress. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning analysis uncovered genes or subsystems that are predictive of differential tolerance in either acid or osmotic stress. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim retained verbatim with the same level of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Physical parameters may supersede interspecies interactions in determining the relative abundance of community members. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts that physical parameters supersede interspecies interactions; that specific comparative claim is absent from the published list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work highlights the physical environment as a major driver of bacterial composition. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides insight into the ability of the microbiota to respond to common perturbations that may be encountered in the gut. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens and narrows the conclusion from asserting the physical environment as a "major driver" to providing insights about response to perturbations, reducing scope and stated confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The growth of specific taxa in limiting conditions in isolation correlated with survival in complex communities in vitro and in an in vivo mouse model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific experimental correlation between isolated growth and survival in communities/in vivo is present in the publication but not in the preprint claims list, so it is an added result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distinct response shapes, termed basis profile curves (BPCs), were uncovered at each recording electrode in the VTC following electrical stimulation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Electrical brain stimulation elicits corticocortical evoked potentials of unique shape and high amplitude in the human ventral temporal cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same primary result about distinct/unique stimulation-evoked response shapes in VTC; wording is equivalent in scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Corticocortical evoked potentials (CCEPs) of unique shape and high amplitude were elicited following stimulation of several brain regions, classified into a set of four consensus BPCs across subjects.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified four consensus basis profile curves (BPCs) that represent distinct response shapes in the ventral temporal cortex following stimulation.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report identification of four consensus BPCs representing distinct response shapes; the publication restates this result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stimulation produced sustained high frequency power decreases and low frequency power increases that spanned multiple BPC categories.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claim set does not mention spectral (high/low frequency power) changes; this preprint result is not reflected in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Characterizing distinct shapes in stimulation responses reveals significant differences in input from cortical and limbic structures to the VTC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings reveal significant differences in input from cortical and limbic structures to the ventral temporal cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about differential cortical vs limbic inputs, but expresses it with somewhat lower stated certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates that single pulse electrical stimulation is an effective tool for understanding the synaptic physiology of stimulation-driven inputs in the VTC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single-pulse electrical stimulation is an effective method for understanding the synaptic physiology of stimulation-driven inputs in the ventral temporal cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological/conceptual claim about single-pulse stimulation being an effective approach; wording and confidence are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We were able to discriminate confidently between HBV genotypes A-H using 10 amino acid sites.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We were able to discriminate confidently between HBV genotypes A-H using ten amino acid sites.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact discriminatory result using ten amino acid sites with equivalent confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RFA analysis is a powerful tool for identifying amino acid sites that predict HBV lineage.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RFA analysis is a powerful tool for identifying amino acid sites that predict the HBV lineage.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same methodological conclusion about RFA's utility for predicting HBV lineage.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An unexpectedly high number of informative sites were found in the spacer domain of the Polymerase gene.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The spacer domain of the Polymerase gene has an unexpectedly high number of informative sites, contrary to its conventional view as unimportant for structure or function.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly restates the unexpected enrichment of informative sites in the spacer domain and adds interpretive context; the core result is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Properties of the amino acids were generally not conserved between genotypes at informative sites.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim among those listed addresses conservation of amino acid properties at informative sites, so this result is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results may have implications for understanding HBV evolution and the role of the spacer domain.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The spacer domain of the Polymerase gene has an unexpectedly high number of informative sites, contrary to its conventional view as unimportant for structure or function.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the spacer domain result in terms of its functional importance (contradicting conventional views), which clarifies and narrows the preprint's general implication about evolutionary/functional roles.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fine-scale functional connectome can predict up to twice the variance in cognition compared to the coarse-scale functional connectome.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The fine-scale functional connectome can predict up to twice the variance in cognition compared to the coarse-scale functional connectome.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative prediction result verbatim; no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual differences in the fine-scale connectome are more reliable than those in the coarse-scale connectome, but they are less heritable.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individual differences in the fine-scale connectome are more reliable than those in the coarse-scale connectome.
alignments.rationale: The publication separates the reliability and heritability aspects into distinct claims; mapping to the reliability claim is closest but the heritability component is treated elsewhere in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The alignment and scale of connectomes influence their ability to predict behavior, with some cognitive traits being equally well predicted by both scales, while less heritable cognitive traits are better predicted by the fine-scale connectome.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The alignment and scale of connectomes influence their ability to predict behavior, with some cognitive traits being equally well predicted by both scales.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the main point about alignment and scale influencing prediction and equality for some traits, while the more specific heritability-linked assertion is reported elsewhere in the publication set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reliable, heritable, and behaviorally-relevant individual differences exist at dissociable scales of the functional connectome.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dissociable individual differences in information processing are represented at different scales of the functional connectome, which have distinct implications for heritability and cognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion about dissociable, scale-dependent individual differences and their implications for heritability and cognition; content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GC neurons primarily encode taste information using a rate code.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GC neurons primarily encode taste information using a rate code.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same primary result about rate coding in GC neurons with equivalent confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The timing of spikes is an important factor in taste discrimination.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The timing of spikes is an important factor in taste discrimination.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same result but with higher stated certainty (medium in preprint vs high in publication), so the expressed confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Taste discrimination using spike timing is improved when the timing of licks is considered in the analysis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Taste discrimination using spike timing is improved when the timing of licks is considered in the analysis.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result with higher stated confidence than the preprint (medium -> high), indicating amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interlick phase of spiking provides more information than the absolute spike timing itself.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The interlick phase of spiking provides more information than the absolute spike timing itself.
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same result but the publication lists it with slightly lower certainty (medium) compared to the preprint (high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability of GC neurons to distinguish among tastes is best when spike rate and timing is interpreted relative to the timing of licks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ability of GC neurons to distinguish among tastes is best when spike rate and timing is interpreted relative to the timing of licks.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same integrative conclusion with equivalent wording and high confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AlphaFold2 assigns confident structures to nearly 15% of human intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs).
alignments.pub_claim_text: AlphaFold2 assigns confident structures to nearly 15% of human intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs).
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative result verbatim; no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AlphaFold2 can identify conditionally folding IDRs at a precision as high as 88% at a 10% false positive rate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AlphaFold2 can identify conditionally folding IDRs at a precision as high as 88% at a 10% false positive rate.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance metric and threshold; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Human disease mutations are nearly 5-fold enriched in conditionally folded IDRs over IDRs in general.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human disease mutations are nearly fivefold enriched in conditionally folded IDRs over IDRs in general.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same enrichment result with equivalent confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Up to 80% of IDRs in prokaryotes are predicted to conditionally fold, compared to less than 20% of eukaryotic IDRs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Up to 80% of IDRs in prokaryotes are predicted to conditionally fold, compared to less than 20% of eukaryotic IDRs.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this comparative prokaryote/eukaryote prediction result unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AlphaFold2 predictions do not reveal functionally relevant structural plasticity within IDRs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AlphaFold2 predictions do not reveal functionally relevant structural plasticity within IDRs.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same limitation but with higher stated certainty (publication lists this as high certainty), so expressed confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fNIRS-based neurofeedback is a feasible method to modulate rTPJ functions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: fNIRS-based neurofeedback is a feasible method to modulate rTPJ functions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same feasibility claim with essentially identical wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The upregulation group maintained increased rTPJ activity, while the downregulation group showed a decline from initial activation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The upregulation group maintained increased rTPJ activity, while the downregulation group showed a decline from initial activation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is reported identically but the publication expresses higher certainty than the preprint, indicating amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a significant group x time interaction effect in the performance of the reorienting of attention task, with decreased reaction times in the upregulation group and increased reaction times in the downregulation group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There were significant group x time interaction effects in the performance of the reorienting of attention task. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both report a significant group x time interaction, but the publication omits the directional detail about reaction time changes present in the preprint, so the claim is revised to be less specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subjects are able to regulate the rTPJ with different learning characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants can learn to actively modulate their rTPJ activity via neurofeedback. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core claim that participants can modulate rTPJ but drops the emphasis on different learning characteristics and expresses lower certainty, so the claim is narrowed and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Findings have important implications for clinical translation of neurofeedback interventions targeting the rTPJ. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not make the stated clinical-translation implication; that specific contribution is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CSDGI is effective to infer driver genes that are cancer subtype-specific. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CSDGI is effective to infer driver genes that are cancer subtype-specific. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same effectiveness claim with the same framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CSDGI is the first method to explore cancer driver genes at the cancer subtype level. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CSDGI is the first method to explore cancer driver genes at the cancer subtype level. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same novelty/contribution claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inferred cancer subtype-specific driver genes (CSDGs) indicate key biological processes and disease pathways. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Functional and disease enrichment analysis shows these inferred CSDGs indicate the key biological processes and disease pathways. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides methodological detail (enrichment analysis) but conveys the same result that inferred CSDGs indicate key processes and pathways, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CSDGI can be a useful method to understand the mechanisms of cell transformation driving tumors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CSDGI can be a useful method to understand the mechanisms of cell transformation driving tumours. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion about CSDGI's potential utility with essentially identical wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CASowary is a novel machine learning and computational tool designed to predict the efficacy of sgRNA for RNA knockdown. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CASowary is a novel machine learning and computational tool designed to predict the efficacy of sgRNA. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication shortens the phrasing slightly (omitting explicit 'for RNA knockdown') but conveys the same contribution, so the claim is clarified with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The noise-normalized accuracy of the CASowary model exceeds 70% after accounting for noise in the training data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The noise-normalized accuracy of the CASowary model exceeds 70% after accounting for noise in the training data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Highly effective sgRNA predictions made by CASowary have been experimentally validated using an independent RNA targeting Cas system -CIRTS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Highly effective sgRNA predictions made by CASowary have been experimentally validated using an independent RNA targeting Cas system – CIRTS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental validation claim is conserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CASowary can predict high quality guides for numerous transcripts in a cell line specific manner using transcriptome-wide protein occupancy maps. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CASowary can predict high quality guides for numerous transcripts in a cell line specific manner using transcriptome-wide protein occupancy maps. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this result with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of CASowary to whole transcriptomes could facilitate the development of therapeutic interventions linked with aberrations in RNA regulatory processes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of CASowary to whole transcriptomes should facilitate the development of therapeutic interventions linked with aberrations in RNA regulatory processes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the language from 'could' to 'should', indicating a modest amplification of confidence while preserving the same conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differences in gut microbial taxa and gene functions are associated with overall cognitive function and with differences in the size of multiple brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differences in microbial taxa and genes are associated with overall cognitive function and the size of brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same association between microbial features and cognition/brain region size with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Many species, including Gordonibacter pamelae and Blautia wexlerae, were significantly associated with higher cognitive function, while Ruminococcus gnavus was more commonly found in children with low cognitive scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Species including Alistipes obesi, Blautia wexlerae, and Ruminococcus gnavus were enriched or depleted in children with higher cognitive function scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both list species associated with cognitive scores and share two taxa (Blautia wexlerae, Ruminococcus gnavus), but the publication's species list differs (e.g., Alistipes obesi replaces Gordonibacter pamelae), so the taxon-level claim is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Microbial genes for enzymes involved in the metabolism of neuroactive compounds, particularly short-chain fatty acids such as acetate and propionate, were associated with cognitive function. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Microbial metabolism of short-chain fatty acids was associated with cognitive function. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the association with SCFA metabolism but drops the broader framing about other neuroactive compound metabolism, so the claim is narrowed/revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models were able to use microbial taxa to predict the volume of brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine models were able to predict the volume of brain regions from microbial profiles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about predictive models using microbial data to estimate brain region volumes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings provide potential biomarkers of neurocognition and brain development and may lead to the future development of targets for early detection and early intervention. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings provide potential biomarkers of neurocognitive development and may enable development of targets for early detection and intervention. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same concluding implication about potential biomarkers and targets for early detection/intervention with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Compartments within the grapevine retain distinct core microbiota regardless of the rootstock to which they are grafted. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Plant compartment had the largest influence on microbial diversity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both emphasize the dominant role of compartment; the publication frames it as the largest influence rather than the absolute 'regardless' phrasing, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rootstock genotypes did not differ significantly in microbial community richness or composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neither rootstock genotype nor irrigation significantly influenced microbial diversity or composition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states the same null effect of rootstock (and irrigation) on diversity/composition, matching the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual microbial taxa exhibited significant differences in abundance based on rootstock and irrigation treatment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differential abundance of bacterial and fungal taxa varied as a function of rootstock and irrigation treatment. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both report taxa-level differential abundance by rootstock and irrigation; the publication expresses this with somewhat lower certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relative abundance of Acetobacterales and Saccharomycetes in the berry was influenced by complex interactions among rootstock genotype and irrigation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Acetobacterales and Saccharomycetes displayed higher relative abundance in berries of grapevines grafted to '1103P' and 'SO4' rootstocks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies particular rootstocks and reports stronger evidence (higher certainty) for higher relative abundance, narrowing the preprint's broader interaction claim and amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further experimental validation is needed to understand how associations with these microorganisms impact a vine’s susceptibility to sour rot and the characteristics of wine. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit call for further experimental validation regarding sour rot susceptibility or wine characteristics, so this limitation statement is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed non-parametric permutation-based statistical framework is designed for neurophysiological data to perform group-level inferences on non-negative measures of information. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed non-parametric permutation-based statistical framework is designed for neurophysiological data to perform group-level inferences on non-negative measures of information. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication statements are effectively identical in content and certainty, describing the same framework and purpose.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework supports both fixed- and random-effect models to adapt to inter-individuals and inter-sessions variability. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using numerical simulations, the accuracy in ground-truth retrieving of both fixed- and random-effect models was compared. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the claim in terms of simulation-based evaluation of fixed- and random-effect models rather than the broader support claim, and expresses lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using numerical simulations, the accuracy in ground-truth retrieving of both group models was compared, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed framework. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using numerical simulations, the accuracy in ground-truth retrieving of both fixed- and random-effect models was compared. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the same simulation-based accuracy comparison, but the publication presents it with more restrained wording and lower reported certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework can extract stereotypical task- and behavior-related effects across the population, covering scales from local brain regions to inter-areal functional connectivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework was used to extract stereotypical task- and behavior-related effects across the population, covering scales from local brain regions to network properties. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result but uses slightly different terminology ('network properties' vs 'inter-areal functional connectivity'), keeping the substantive claim and certainty intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An open-source Python toolbox called Frites includes the proposed statistical pipeline using information-theoretic metrics for cognitive brain network extraction. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An open-source Python toolbox called Frites was presented, which includes the proposed statistical pipeline using information-theoretic metrics. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same toolbox and pipeline with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The initial step of FGF2 oligomerization on membrane surfaces depends on C95-C95 disulfide-bridge-mediated FGF2 dimerization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The initial step of FGF2 translocation across the plasma membrane depends on C95-C95 disulfide-bridge-mediated FGF2 dimerization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts emphasis from oligomerization on membranes to translocation across the plasma membrane, altering the mechanistic focus while preserving the role of C95-C95 dimerization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FGF2 with a C95A substitution is defective in oligomerization, pore formation, and membrane translocation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: FGF2 with a C95A substitution is defective in oligomerization, pore formation, and membrane translocation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is stated identically in the publication with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: C77 is not involved in FGF2 oligomerization but is part of the protein-protein interaction interface for binding to the α1 subunit of the Na,K-ATPase. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: C77 is not involved in FGF2 oligomerization but is part of the interaction interface for binding to the α1 subunit of the Na,K-ATPase. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding but reports it with reduced certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We propose a mechanism by which C95-C95 bridged FGF2 dimers trigger autocrine and paracrine FGF2 signaling. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: C95-C95-bridged FGF2 dimers are hypothesized to trigger autocrine and paracrine FGF2 signaling. (certainty=low, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the proposed mechanism but frames it more tentatively (lower certainty), effectively clarifying it as a hypothesis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A method has been developed for predicting exosomal proteins with high precision. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A hybrid method combining motif-based and ML-based approaches for predicting exosomal proteins achieved a maximum AUROC of 0.85 and MCC of 0.56 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific performance metrics for the developed prediction method (hybrid), which clarifies the general preprint claim about a high-precision prediction method.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hybrid model achieves a maximum AUROC of 0.85 and MCC of 0.56 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A hybrid method combining motif-based and ML-based approaches for predicting exosomal proteins achieved a maximum AUROC of 0.85 and MCC of 0.56 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance claim for the hybrid model with the same metrics and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the ML-based models improved significantly to AUROC of 0.73 when evolutionary information was used. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning-based models developed using compositional and evolutionary features of proteins achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristics (AUROC) of 0.73. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance (AUROC=0.73) when evolutionary features are included, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A wide range of sequence motifs have been discovered in exosomal proteins. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Exosomal proteins have a variety of sequence-based motifs which can be used to predict exosomal proteins. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same observation about motif variety and extends it to a predictive conclusion, slightly reframing the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A web server and standalone software ExoProPred have been developed for the scientific community. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding publication claim about a web server or standalone ExoProPred is present in the provided publication claim set, so this contribution appears absent from the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Digital holotomography (DHTM) enables real-time, label-free monitoring of the concentration-dependent effects of ibuprofen on red blood cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Digital holotomography (DHTM) enables real-time, label-free monitoring of the concentration-dependent effects of ibuprofen on red blood cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the identical methodological/result claim with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At low concentrations of 0.25-0.50 mM, ibuprofen induces transient morphological changes in red blood cells, while at high concentrations of 1.5-3 mM, the changes are sustained for up to 1.5 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At low concentrations of 0.25-0.50 mM, ibuprofen induces a transient morphological change in red blood cells, while at high concentrations (1-3 mM), the spiculated red blood cells remain altered for up to 1.5 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report the same dose-dependent morphological effects; the publication adjusts the high-concentration range slightly and specifies 'spiculated' RBCs, clarifying the observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Molecular simulations indicate that high concentrations of ibuprofen significantly disrupt the structural integrity and lipid order of red blood cell membranes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Molecular simulations indicate that high concentrations of ibuprofen significantly disrupt the structural integrity and lipid order of red blood cell membranes, whereas low concentrations have a negligible effect. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the explicit contrast that low concentrations have negligible effect, refining the simulation outcome while preserving the main high-concentration result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Control experiments demonstrated that urea, hydrogen peroxide, and aqueous solutions do not induce spicule formation on red blood cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Control experiments demonstrated that urea, hydrogen peroxide, and aqueous solutions do not induce spicule formation on red blood cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical control experiment finding presented with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that DHTM can be utilized as a platform for diagnosing blood disorders and monitoring drug effects in a cost-effective manner. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study clarifies the dose-dependent chemical effects of over-the-counter drugs on red blood cells, which can aid in the rapid detection of drug overdosage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The frontoparietal network (FPN) acts as a dynamic, global coordinator of goal-relevant information.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same functional role for the FPN; the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cingulo-opercular network (CON) transiently disbands to lend processing resources to other goal-relevant networks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cingulo-opercular network (CON) transiently disbands to lend processing resources to other goal-relevant networks.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same observation about the CON; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The FPN and CON have distinct but complementary roles in controlling global network reconfigurations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The FPN and CON have distinct but complementary roles in controlling global network reconfigurations.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion regarding complementary roles; claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study reveals a dissociation between the FPN and CON, suggesting complementary mechanisms underlying goal-directed cognition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study reveals a dissociation between the FPN and CON, suggesting complementary mechanisms underlying goal-directed cognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the dissociation and its implication for goal-directed cognition; the claim remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The parametrized computational framework can predict developmental trajectories based on cell-cell communication networks and initial conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The parameterized computational framework can predict developmental trajectories based on a cell-cell communication network and initial conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same claim about the parameterized framework predicting trajectories with nearly identical wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can qualitatively predict experimental results from a testing set that varied genetic network parameters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The parameterized model can qualitatively predict experimental results from a testing set of in vitro data that varied the genetic network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same predictive claim but clarifies the testing set as in vitro data, making the claim more specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework can recommend novel network implementations for the formation of a 4-layered structure that has not been reported previously. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed framework could serve as a testing ground to identify the reachable space of morphologies obtainable by controlling contact-dependent cell-cell communications and adhesion. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The design method for synthetic genetic programs was limited by a trial and error approach, restricting the exploration of possible circuits and parameter ranges. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding limitation about trial-and-error design appears in the publication claims; the limitation is not restated or addressed directly.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The synthetic genetic programs based on cell-cell contact-dependent signaling using synthetic Notch pathways can drive the formation of multilayered spheroids by modulating cell-cell adhesion. (certainty=high, type=result)
