alignments.preprint_claim_text: The associated code base for the framework is openly available and flexible enough to be extended with new methods. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims list does not mention availability or extensibility of the code base, so this contribution is not carried forward.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The construction of perturbative maps of biology enables the generation of genome-scale sets of pairwise comparisons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The explicit phrasing about generating genome-scale sets of pairwise comparisons appears in the publication but was not stated in the preprint claims, so it is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LDA methods, including a novel gradient descent-based multiclass harmonic variant (GDHLDA), outperform PCA in class separation for identifying optimal collective variables (CVs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LDA methods, including a novel gradient descent-based multiclass harmonic variant (GDHLDA), outperform PCA in class separation for identifying optimal collective variables (CVs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication text are essentially identical in wording and meaning about GDHLDA outperforming PCA for CV identification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identified CVs include features previously associated with conformational transitions in the MFSD2A lysolipid transporter. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The identified CVs included features previously associated with conformational transitions in the MFSD2A lysolipid transporter. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same result that identified CVs overlap established conformational features in MFSD2A.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conformational shifts in transmembrane helix 7 and in residue Y294 on this helix are critical features discriminating the metastable states in MFSD2A. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Conformational shifts in transmembrane helix 7 and in residue Y294 on this helix are critical features discriminating the metastable states in MFSD2A. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about helix 7 and residue Y294 is carried through identically from preprint to publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated detection of LDA-based CVs enhances the interpretive power of molecular dynamics simulations for understanding complex biological systems. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated detection of LDA-based CVs is effective in extracting functional relevance from MD trajectories to drive biased MD simulations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the general interpretive benefit into a more specific, actionable use (extracting functional relevance to drive biased MD), which clarifies and narrows the original, broader conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We trained a supervised classification model that can accurately classify the organ-of-origin for a plant transcriptome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A supervised classification model was trained to accurately classify the organ-of-origin for a plant transcriptome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about training an accurate organ-of-origin classifier with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The genome-wide, organ-delimited gene regulatory networks (OD-GRNs) identified de novo many known regulators of organ development and processes operating in those organs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The genome-wide, organ-delimited gene regulatory networks (OD-GRNs) recalled many known regulators of organ development and processes operating in those organs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both texts convey that OD-GRNs recovered/recall many known regulators; wording differs slightly but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We validated that seven more candidate transcription factors, whose role was previously unknown in seed lipid biosynthesis, indeed affect this process. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study validated MybS2, TGA4, SPL12, AGL18, and DiV2 as regulators of seed lipid biosynthesis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a specific (and smaller) list of validated TFs compared to the preprint's statement of validating seven candidates, indicating a revision and narrowing of the original numerical claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach described in this study is broadly applicable across any organism that has a large body of public gene expression data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate or support the broad, organism-agnostic applicability claim; instead it adds different downstream mechanistic claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The molecular mechanism of MybS2 was elucidated, showing that it induces purple acid phosphatase family genes and lipid synthesis genes to enhance seed lipid content. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This mechanistic claim about MybS2 is new in the publication and was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new technique called 'Canonical Response Parameterization' (CRP) simplifies the study of cortico-cortical evoked potential (CCEP) shapes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The new technique called 'Canonical Response Parameterization' (CRP) simplifies the study of cortico-cortical evoked potential (CCEP) shapes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is described identically in both versions, maintaining the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CRP allows for the comparison of voltage response traces of different shapes by quantifying cross-projection magnitudes, response duration, and canonical shape projection amplitudes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CRP allows for direct comparison of dissimilar trace shapes from different brain areas by quantifying various parameters such as cross-projection magnitudes and response duration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same capability (comparison via quantified parameters); differences are minor wording adjustments without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method enables the identification and rejection of artifactual trials by detecting outliers in sub-distributions of cross-projection magnitude. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artifactual trials in CCEP measurements can be automatically identified and rejected using the proposed technique. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert automatic identification and rejection of artifactual trials; the publication states it slightly more generally but not contradictory.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This technique facilitates the quantification of statistical significance, signal-to-noise ratio, explained variance, and average voltage of the response. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly enumerate these statistical metrics as a standalone claim; instead it focuses on parameterization and broader applicability, so the specific metric-list claim from the preprint is not retained.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The parameterization technique can be applied in a wide range of other settings involving event-triggered data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a broader applicability claim beyond the preprint that was not present previously.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PanINs are surrounded by the same subtypes of cancer associated fibroblasts present in invasive PDACs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), including antigen-presenting CAFs, are located close to pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasias (PanINs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the proximal association of CAF subtypes with PanINs; wording differs but the biological observation is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new experimental and computational protocol for ST analysis suggests a biological model in which CAF-PanIN interactions promote inflammatory signaling in neoplastic cells transitioning to proliferative signaling as PanINs progress to PDAC. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a transition from CAF-related inflammatory signaling to cellular proliferation during PanIN progression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the transition more directly and with higher certainty, clarifying the model described in the preprint and increasing confidence in the observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of imaging, ST, and scRNA-seq data enables the analysis of cellular and molecular progression from PanINs to PDAC. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study introduces a new imaging, spatial transcriptomics (ST), and single-cell RNA-sequencing integration pipeline to characterize neoplastic cell state transitions during tumorigenesis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the same integration pipeline and its purpose to characterize cell-state transitions; phrasing is equivalent in scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transcriptional profiles of annotated cells show that PanINs are predominantly of the classical PDAC subtype. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not report the asserted predominance of the classical PDAC subtype in PanINs, so this specific transcriptional-subtype claim is not present.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The semi-supervised learning framework for spatial multi-omics has broad applicability across cancer types to decipher the spatiotemporal dynamics of carcinogenesis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a broader applicability claim for the framework that was not explicitly made in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gut microbiota of Alzheimer’s disease continuum patients varies considerably throughout the disease stages. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates that the gut microbiota can be stratified across the Alzheimer's disease continuum using various machine learning techniques. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the variability observation as a demonstrable stratification using ML methods, clarifying how variability can be operationalized; the core idea of variation across stages remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Four distinct machine learning techniques converge on Prevotella and Bacteroides partitioning of the gut microbiota across the Alzheimer’s disease continuum. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prevotella and Bacteroides are identified as consensus partitions in the gut microbiota of patients across the Alzheimer's disease continuum. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both texts state that Prevotella/Bacteroides partitioning is a consensus finding across methods; wording is slightly different but meaning is identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Signature taxa and neuropsychometric parameters robustly classify the heterogeneous groups within the cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not explicitly assert the combined taxa-plus-neuropsychometric classifier performance as stated in the preprint, so this specific performance claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is an unmet need for microbiota-informed stratification of Alzheimer’s disease clinical cohorts in nutritional studies. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research highlights the importance of microbiota-informed stratification of Alzheimer's disease clinical cohorts for future intervention studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's identified unmet need as an important consideration for future studies, but with slightly lower stated certainty, tempering the original phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides a framework for precision nutrition approaches and diet-based intervention studies targeting Alzheimer’s disease cohorts. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides a framework for precision nutrition approaches aimed at modulating the gut microbiota in Alzheimer's disease management. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the contribution about a precision-nutrition framework but expresses it with somewhat lower certainty and a more specific management-oriented framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual speeds of forgetting are associated with resting-state connectivity within the default mode network (DMN) as well as between the DMN and cortical sensory areas.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individual speeds of forgetting are associated with resting-state connectivity within the default mode network (DMN) as well as between the DMN and cortical sensory areas.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same association claim with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual speeds of forgetting were predicted with high accuracy (r = .78) from connectivity patterns alone.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individual speeds of forgetting were predicted with high accuracy (r = .77) from connectivity patterns alone.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same predictive result with a slightly different/updated correlation value (minor numerical clarification).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings suggest that forgetting reflects how well memory traces are preserved in the brain, not just whether they can be retrieved.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings provide evidence for the hypothesis that forgetting is a result of storage degradation, rather than of retrieval failure.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's interpretive claim more directly as support for storage-degradation (retrieval vs storage distinction), clarifying the mechanism implied by the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sixty-four out of 131 preclinical imaging features were identified as robust.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sixty-four out of 131 preclinical imaging features were identified as robust.
alignments.rationale: Exact same result reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NB-RadSig performed highest in predicting and assessing response to therapy in the preclinical PDX trial.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NB-RadSig performed highest in predicting and assessing response to therapy in the preclinical PDX trial.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim carried to publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of SVM-RadSig and NB-RadSig to predict and assess response was practically identical and superior to SUV mean, SUV max, and SUL peak measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of SVM-RadSig and NB-RadSig to predict and assess response was practically identical and superior to SUVmean, SUVmax, and SULpeak measures.
alignments.rationale: Same comparative performance claim, only minor formatting of measure names changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We optimized robust FDG-PET radiomic signatures (RadSig) to predict and assess response to therapy in a context of a co-clinical imaging trial.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We optimized robust FDG-PET radiomic signatures (RadSig) to predict and assess response to therapy in the context of a co-clinical imaging trial.
alignments.rationale: Conclusion and scope are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gene panel-based models can accurately predict antibiotic mechanism of action and the fitness outcome of Streptococcus pneumoniae in the presence of antibiotics or under nutrient depletion.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report gene-panel-specific models or the Streptococcus pneumoniae-specific claim; emphasis shifts to entropy-based, generalizable methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transcriptomic entropy can be used as a generalizable stress signature for predicting bacterial fitness across multiple species and stress conditions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Entropy-based predictions have been validated in 7 species under antibiotic and non-antibiotic conditions.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides validation of the preprint's generalizable entropy-as-stress-signature claim across multiple species and conditions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An entropy-based model was developed and validated to predict the minimum inhibitory concentration of an antibiotic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using entropy as a single feature allows for fitness and quantitative antibiotic sensitivity predictions that generalize well beyond training data.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports quantitative antibiotic-sensitivity predictions from entropy, which corresponds to the preprint's MIC-prediction claim (same functional result and generalization emphasis).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inclusion of a varied set of multi-omics features enhances fitness predictions by reducing ambiguity.
alignments.rationale: The published paper emphasizes entropy as a single, generalizable feature and does not report the claimed benefit of multi-omics inclusion, so this preprint claim is not supported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work establishes the fundamentals for potentially new approaches in infectious disease diagnostics, including antibiotic susceptibility testing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work demonstrates the feasibility of universal predictions of bacterial fitness, establishing fundamentals for new approaches in infectious disease diagnostics.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the translational conclusion with stronger language and broader validation, increasing confidence in the diagnostic/AST implications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BioMedical Evidence Graph (BMEG) contains over 36 million vertices and 29 million edges.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The construction of the BioMedical Evidence Graph (BMEG) has resulted in a graph containing over 41 million vertices and 57 million edges.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports updated, larger graph size metrics compared to the preprint (numeric revision of the same claim).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BMEG system provides a graph query based API that enables analysis of cancer biology data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BMEG system provides a graph query-based application programming interface that enables analysis across various data sets.
alignments.rationale: Same functional claim about the graph-query API enabling cross-dataset analysis, carried into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BMEG allows for integrated analysis by connecting sample level molecular information to reference knowledge bases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BMEG has demonstrated utility in cross-data set analysis, including mutation significance analysis and drug-response machine learning.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete examples of the integrated-analysis utility asserted in the preprint, clarifying the kinds of analyses enabled.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BMEG is unique in the scale of the graph and the type of data it makes available compared to other integrated graph systems.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BMEG is unique in the scale of the graph and the type of data it makes available compared to other integrated graph systems.
alignments.rationale: The uniqueness claim is preserved in the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm scored SWDs along a continuum that is highly correlated with human confidence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed SVM-based algorithm for automated detection of SWDs in a mouse model of absence epilepsy scores SWDs along a continuum that is highly correlated with human confidence.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about continuum scoring and its correlation with human confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated method provides a framework for the quantitative description of SWDs and their relation to other electrographic events.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm allows for more effective characterization of ambiguous SWD events by providing confidence scores.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the algorithm's practical utility in characterizing ambiguous events via confidence scores, a clearer framing of the preprint's proposed framework.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Events along the scoring continuum generated by the algorithm are temporally and proportionately correlated with changes in spectral power bands relevant to behavioral states including sleep.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Events scored along the continuum are temporally and proportionately correlated with abrupt changes in spectral power bands relevant to normal behavioral states.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same correlation with spectral power but uses slightly different wording (e.g., 'abrupt changes' and 'normal behavioral states'), clarifying the observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrates advantages over human scoring in terms of consistency and rapidity in detecting SWDs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study contributes to the need for continued development of SWD detection methods specifically in mice.
alignments.rationale: The publication tones down the strong comparative-advantage claim and instead frames the work as contributing to method development, reducing confidence and changing emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Insects uniquely control sexual differentiation through sex-specific RNA splicing involving the doublesex (dsx) transcription factor and the transformer (tra) splicing factor.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Insects are the only known animals in which sexual differentiation is controlled by sex-specific splicing.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert the uniqueness of sex-specific splicing controlling sexual differentiation in insects and reference the same mechanism; the publication restates the preprint claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functional Tra protein is limited to females in the kissing bug Rhodnius prolixus, but is present in both sexes in the louse Pediculus humanus and the cockroach Blattella germanica.
alignments.pub_claim_text: dsx splicing depends on transformer, which is alternatively spliced to produce functional Tra only in females.
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports species variation in Tra presence across sexes, while the publication presents a generalized female-only functional Tra statement; this changes (narrows) the species-level interpretation and amplifies the universality of female-limited Tra.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In Blattella germanica, RNAi experiments demonstrate that dsx is necessary for male sexual differentiation, while tra controls female development via a dsx-independent pathway.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tra and dsx genes have distinct functions in basal, hemimetabolous insect orders, indicating different stages in the evolution of sexual differentiation mechanisms.
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the species-specific functional result (Blattella) to distinct functions across basal hemimetabolous orders, broadening the scope while preserving the core finding of distinct tra/dsx roles.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The distinctive insect mechanism based on the tra-dsx splicing cascade evolved in a gradual, mosaic process.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The canonical insect tra-dsx pathway evolved through the merger of expanding dsx function and narrowing tra function.
alignments.rationale: Both claims address evolutionary origin of the tra-dsx pathway, but the publication specifies a particular mechanism (merger of expanding dsx and narrowing tra), revising the more general 'gradual, mosaic' description into a specific model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cdc42 is involved in the pattern formation of rosettes during frustrated phagocytosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cdc42 is involved in the pattern formation of rosettes around IgG disks during frustrated phagocytosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds experimental context (around IgG disks) but preserves the same result that Cdc42 is involved in rosette pattern formation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The addition of negative feedback and minor changes to existing reaction-diffusion models can generate the experimentally observed rosette pattern of podosomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The addition of negative feedback and minor changes to existing reaction-diffusion models can generate the experimentally observed rosette pattern of podosomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the modeling result verbatim, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rosette formation can occur through two mechanisms: one involving an initial ring of high activity and the other relying on spatial gradients of intermediate chemical species.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pattern formation can occur through two mechanisms: one involving an intermediate species forming a ring of high activity, and the other relying on spatial gradients of intermediate chemical species.
alignments.rationale: Both descriptions present the same two mechanisms (ring formation vs. gradient-driven), with only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models analyzed suggest experiments to test the validity of the proposed mechanisms for rosette formation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models analyzed suggest experiments to test their validity.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication state that the models motivate experiments; the publication shortens the phrasing but retains the same contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multivariate regression model incorporating auditory naming-related high gamma augmentation (HGA) predicted postoperative changes in Core Language Score (CLS) and Expressive Language Index (ELI).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multivariate regression model incorporating auditory naming-related high gamma augmentation predicted postoperative changes in Core Language Score with r2 of 0.37 and in Expressive Language Index with r2 of 0.32.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same predictive claim but adds quantitative model performance (r2 values), clarifying the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher HGA at the resected language-dominant hemispheric area predicted a more severe postoperative decline in CLS and ELI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher high gamma augmentation at the resected language-dominant hemispheric area predicted a more severe postoperative decline in Core Language Score and Expressive Language Index.
alignments.rationale: This result is restated in the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning-based prediction model indicated that naming-related HGA most strongly contributed to predicting patients showing a greater than 5-point CLS decline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning-based prediction model suggested that naming-related high gamma augmentation most strongly contributed to the improved prediction of patients showing a >5-point Core Language Score decline.
alignments.rationale: Both texts attribute the strongest contribution in the ML model to naming-related HGA for predicting >5-point CLS decline; wording is slightly different but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The auditory naming-based model predicted patients who developed the CLS decline with an accuracy of 0.80.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The auditory naming-based model predicted patients who had a postoperative language decline with an accuracy of 0.80.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the accuracy result (0.80) for the auditory naming-based model with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Virtual resection of an ESM-defined language site would have increased the relative risk of CLS decline by 5.28.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the virtual resection relative risk estimate; this specific quantitative claim appears to be omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inhibiting excitatory neurotransmission does not monotonically regulate dendritic spine density.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inhibiting excitatory neurotransmission does not monotonically regulate dendritic spine density.
alignments.rationale: The result is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Partial inhibition of synaptic transmissions increased spine density, while complete inhibition reduced it.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The biphasic structural plasticity rule maintains neural activity homeostasis under stimulation and permits either synapse formation or synapse loss depending on the degree of activity deprivation.
alignments.rationale: Both describe a biphasic outcome (formation vs loss) depending on activity deprivation; the publication reframes the experimental observation as a structural-plasticity rule but does not change the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A biphasic, spine-number-based homeostatic structural plasticity (HSP) rule reconciled previously divergent experimental findings regarding activity-dependent changes in spine density.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The interaction between homeostatic synaptic scaling and homeostatic structural plasticity provides a mechanistic explanation for divergent findings in the literature.
alignments.rationale: The preprint attributes reconciliation to HSP alone, while the publication attributes it to the interaction between HSP and synaptic scaling, representing a revision of the proposed mechanism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interplay between homeostatic structural plasticity and synaptic scaling is necessary for maintaining firing rate homeostasis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The interaction between homeostatic synaptic scaling and homeostatic structural plasticity provides a mechanistic explanation for divergent findings in the literature.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes that the interaction explains divergent findings rather than explicitly asserting necessity for firing rate homeostasis, so the claim is presented with somewhat tempered emphasis and clarified framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both plasticity rules are driven by intracellular calcium concentration, which reflects cumulative neural activity.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly states that intracellular calcium drives both plasticity rules, so this mechanistic statement appears omitted in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cell density assessed by histology poorly explained intra-tumor variability at the mesoscopic level in mean diffusivity (MD) or fractional anisotropy (FA IP).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cell density assessed by histology poorly explained intra-tumor variability of mean diffusivity (MD) at the mesoscopic level.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the key result that cell density poorly explains mesoscopic MD variability; the preprint additionally mentioned FA in the clause but the core MD-focused result is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cell density and structure anisotropy account for variability in MD and FA IP across tumors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Across tumors, cell density and structure anisotropy were associated with MD and FA<sub>IP</sub> with R<sup>2</sup> values of 0.60 and 0.81, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies the associations (R2 values), clarifying and strengthening the preprint's descriptive claim about the association of cell density and structure anisotropy with MD and FA_IP.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In 37% of the samples, cell density did not explain intra-tumor variability of MD when compared to the degree explained by the convolutional neural network.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In 37% of the samples, cell density did not explain intra-tumor variability of MD when compared to the degree explained by the convolutional neural network.
alignments.rationale: This specific quantitative observation is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tumor vascularization, psammoma bodies, microcysts, and tissue cohesivity were associated with bias in MD prediction when solely cell density was considered.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Features beyond cell density need to be considered when interpreting MD.
alignments.rationale: The preprint lists specific histological features linked to bias; the publication summarizes this finding as a general recommendation to consider features beyond cell density, clarifying and generalizing the point.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Features beyond cell density need to be considered when interpreting MD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Features beyond cell density need to be considered when interpreting MD.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion directly from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: R0 for rubella is typically low, with values of <5, 5-10, and >10 for 81, 14, and 3 settings respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: R0 for rubella was <5, 5-10 and >10 for 81, 14 and 3 settings respectively, with no apparent regional differences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric R0 breakdown is identical; the publication adds an explicit note about lack of regional differences, which clarifies but does not change the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: R0 was most correlated with educational attainment and household indicators, although the correlation was relatively weak. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: R0 was most correlated with educational attainment and household indicators, although the correlation for each was relatively weak. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same correlations with only minor wording changes; meaning and confidence are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A random forest regression algorithm did not perform better in predicting R0 than simple linear regression or a regional averaging approach. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A random forest did not perform better in predicting R0 than simple linear regression or a regional averaging approach. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The methods comparison and conclusion are the same in both versions with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using indicators to estimate R0 is not straightforward, and a regional averaging approach may provide as reliable an estimate for settings lacking seroprevalence data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using indicators to estimate R0 for rubella is not straightforward. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the point that indicator-based estimation is not straightforward but omits the regional-averaging suggestion present in the preprint, narrowing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings may be relevant for other infections and studies estimating the disease burden and the impact of interventions for settings lacking seroprevalence data. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding claim about broader applicability to other infections appears in the publication's top claims, so this contribution appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A regional averaging approach may provide as reliable an estimate of R0 for settings lacking seroprevalence data as one based on indicators. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific phrasing of the regional-averaging-as-reliable alternative appears as a separate publication claim distinct from the matched preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GenePy is a gene score that transforms sequencing data to estimate whole-gene pathogenicity on a per-patient basis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenePy is a novel gene-level scoring system that transforms next-generation sequencing data interpretation from variant-level to gene-level. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the same gene-level scoring concept; the publication wording reframes interpretation but does not change the core contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GenePy sensitively identifies known causal genes, as demonstrated by calculating GenePy scores for NOD2 in a cohort of patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenePy scores demonstrate markedly more significant results in discriminating genes associated with common complex diseases compared to the most commonly applied association tool. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports a specific demonstration (NOD2); the publication presents a broader performance claim across complex diseases, indicating a shift from a specific demo to a more general framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GenePy demonstrates markedly more significant results in identifying causal genes compared to the most commonly applied tool for combining common and rare variation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenePy scores demonstrate markedly more significant results in discriminating genes associated with common complex diseases compared to the most commonly applied association tool. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core comparative performance claim versus the commonly used tool is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The per gene-per individual nature of GenePy transforms the utility of sequencing data and facilitates downstream data integration. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GenePy scores are suitable for downstream data integration with transcriptomic and proteomic data that report at the gene level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims state suitability for downstream integration; the publication specifies integration targets (transcriptomic/proteomic), clarifying the conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The majority of genes generate GenePy scores less than 0.01, although individuals with multiple rare highly deleterious mutations can accumulate extremely high GenePy scores. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This distributional result about GenePy score values is present in the publication but was not among the preprint's listed top claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We propose a method for identifying High Attention (HA) sites at critical residues from the middle layers of the PLM. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We propose a method for identifying High Attention (HA) sites at critical residues from the middle layers of the Protein Language Model (PLM). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These HA sites provide interpretable links to biological functions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These HA sites provide interpretable links to biological functions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about interpretability is present verbatim and retains the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HA sites improve active site predictions for functions of unannotated proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HA sites improve active site predictions for functions of unannotated proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The predictive improvement claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We make available the HA sites for the human proteome. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's top claims do not mention making HA sites for the human proteome available, so this specific data-release claim appears omitted from the listed publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This work offers a broadly applicable approach to protein classification and functional annotation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a general concluding claim about broader applicability that was not present in the preprint's top claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DRUIDom can predict bio-interactions between drug candidate compounds and target proteins by utilizing the domain modularity of proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DRUIDom identifies bio-interactions between drug candidate compounds and targets by utilizing the domain modularity of proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is preserved with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method led to 27,032 high-confidence associations between 250 domains and 8,165 compounds, resulting in approximately 5 million new compound–protein interactions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method led to 27,032 high-confidence associations between 250 domains and 8,165 compounds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core counted associations but omits the preprint's extrapolated figure of ~5 million interactions, narrowing the reported result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LIMK-inhibitor-2 and its derivatives significantly block cancer cell migration through inhibition of LIMK phosphorylation and the downstream protein cofilin. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LIMK-inhibitor-2 and its derivatives significantly block cancer cell migration through inhibition of LIMK phosphorylation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same experimental effect but omits explicit mention of the downstream target cofilin in the top-level claim, slightly narrowing the mechanistic detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One of the derivative compounds (LIMKi-2d) was identified as a promising candidate due to its action on resistant Mahlavu liver cancer cells. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: One of the derivative compounds (LIMKi-2d) was identified as a promising candidate due to its action on resistant Mahlavu liver cancer cells. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific candidate identification and rationale are preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DRUIDom produces novel and biologically relevant results that can be directly used in the early steps of the drug discovery process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's top claims do not explicitly state this applied drug-discovery utility, so the preprint's concluding claim appears omitted from the listed publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: DRUIDom was experimentally validated by synthesizing and analyzing compounds predicted to target LIM-kinase proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit experimental-validation claim that was not listed among the preprint's top claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models based on the NeMo Tool’s estimates of structural connectivity (SC) and functional connectivity (FC) better classified people with multiple sclerosis (pwMS) by disability level than SC and FC observed directly using advanced MRI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models based on the NeMo Tool's estimates of SC and FC better classified pwMS by disability level than SC and FC observed directly in the individual using advanced MRI. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core comparative classification result is preserved in the publication with equivalent certainty; only phrasing and labeling as a conclusion differ.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Estimated SC and FC generally outperformed observed SC and FC in classifying pwMS into no disability vs evidence of disability groups, with regional estimated SC and FC having the best performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The regional eSC and eFC models outperformed their observed FC and SC counterparts in classifying pwMS into disability categories. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements emphasize that regional estimated connectivity models outperform observed counterparts; the publication frames it succinctly but preserves meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increased estimated FC node strength of regions in the visual network was associated with evidence of disability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Decreased estimated SC node strength in default mode and ventral attention networks and increased eFC node strength in visual networks was associated with evidence of disability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication combines related regional associations into one claim; the visual-network eFC increase from the preprint is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Decreased estimated SC node strength of regions in the default mode and ventral attention networks was associated with evidence of disability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Decreased estimated SC node strength in default mode and ventral attention networks and increased eFC node strength in visual networks was associated with evidence of disability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication bundles both SC decreases and eFC increases into a single result; the SC decreases for those networks match the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feature weights for the regional estimated FC and the regional observed FC classification models were significantly correlated. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's top claims do not report the feature-weight correlation, so this specific model-interpretation claim appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This work provides a viable alternative to performing high-cost, advanced MRI in patient populations. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit contribution about offering an alternative to costly advanced MRI that was not stated among the preprint's listed claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The process of ipsi- and contra-lateral signal integration in auditory localization is highly adaptive and depends strongly on recent stimulus statistics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The process of ipsi- and contra-lateral signal integration in auditory localization is highly adaptive and depends strongly on recent stimulus statistics. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim wording with the same meaning, though the reported certainty is reduced from high to medium, so the claim content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed neural model demonstrates that a single LSO neuron quickly adapts to stimulus statistics, allowing it to encode an extended range of interaural level differences (ILDs) in the ipsilateral hemisphere. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed neural model demonstrates that a single LSO neuron quickly adapts to stimulus statistics and can encode an extended range of interaural level differences (ILDs) in the ipsilateral hemisphere. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent certainty and scope, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mechanisms of temporal competitive-cooperative interaction and local feedback jointly sensitize the circuit to enable a response shift towards contra-lateral and ipsi-lateral stimuli. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mechanisms of temporal competitive-cooperative interaction and the local feedback mechanism jointly sensitize the circuit to enable a response shift towards contra-lateral and ipsi-lateral stimuli. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains essentially the same mechanistic conclusion with equivalent phrasing and certainty, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The incorporation of an innovative adaptation mechanism facilitates a shift in neural responses towards the most effective stimulus range based on recent stimulus history. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating the innovative adaptation mechanism facilitates a shift in neural responses towards the most effective stimulus range based on recent stimulus history. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same result about the adaptation mechanism and its effect on response shift with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model provides a unique measurement of the adaptation efficacy of LSO neurons, which is crucial for normal function. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to this specific claim about providing a unique measurement of adaptation efficacy and its necessity for normal function, so it appears omitted in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Immunoglobulin fold (Ig-fold) accounts for 2 to 3% of protein coding regions in the human genome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Immunoglobulin fold (Ig-fold) represents the most populous fold in the human genome, comprising 2 to 3% of protein coding regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the 2–3% figure but adds that the Ig-fold is the most populous fold, expanding the claim's framing (clarification/broadening) while keeping high certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The IgStrand numbering scheme enables the identification and annotation of Ig structural proteomes and interactomes across species. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The IgStrand numbering scheme enables the identification of Ig structural proteomes and interactomes across species. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this contribution claim verbatim with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Ig-fold is a fundamental building block in many protein families across species, providing stability and adaptability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The scheme reveals structural homologies among protein structures that extend beyond currently identified Ig- and Ig-like domain variants. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes broader structural homologies related to Ig domains, which clarifies/extends the preprint's general claim about Ig as a widespread building block, though wording and emphasis differ.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The IgStrand numbering scheme facilitates detailed comparative structural, functional, and evolutionary analyses of Ig-domain variants. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The IgStrand numbering scheme lays the foundation for the systematic annotation of structural proteomes and enhances machine learning applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The systematic annotation of Ig-domains can assist in the rational design of Ig-based therapeutics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not include a direct assertion about assisting rational therapeutic design, so this preprint claim appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The robust Ig protein folding algorithm used by nature forms beta sandwich supersecondary structures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim (about nature's folding forming beta-sandwich structures) has no counterpart in the preprint and is therefore an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SR models yielded a higher Pearson correlation to bone morphological parameters on the ex vivo test set compared to the use of a conventional image processing pipeline. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The super-resolution (SR) models predicted the bone morphological parameters on the ex vivo test set more accurately than conventional image processing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same quantitative result about improved accuracy on ex vivo bone morphology with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Utilizing experimental laboratory imaging modalities in model training could allow pushing the spatial resolution limit beyond state-of-the-art clinical musculoskeletal and dental CBCT imaging. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model training with laboratory modalities could push the resolution limit beyond state-of-the-art clinical musculoskeletal and dental CBCT. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about laboratory modalities enabling higher resolution with similar certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SR in dental imaging allowed to visualize smaller mineralized structures in the maxillofacial region, however, some artifacts were observed near the crown of the teeth. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Musculoskeletal and maxillofacial CBCT images showed more details on SR than interpolation, but artifacts were observed near the crown of the teeth. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same empirical observation about increased detail and artifacts near tooth crowns; content and certainty align closely.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predicted grayscale values of line pair patterns exceeded those of uniform areas. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The phantom analysis confirmed higher spatial resolution on the SR images than interpolation, although image grayscales were modified. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports that grayscales were modified in phantom analysis, which clarifies the preprint's specific observation about line-pair grayscale behavior rather than directly repeating it.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A larger maxillofacial dataset would be recommended for training SR models in dental applications. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A larger maxillofacial training dataset is recommended for dental applications. (certainty=medium, type=recommendation)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same recommendation but reports it with higher stated certainty (medium vs low), so content is unchanged but confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Human neural stem cell (hNSC)-derived neurons and astrocytes are highly susceptible to infection with the virulent rabies virus strain Tha and the less virulent strain Th2P-4M in vitro. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RABV replicates in human stem cell-derived neurons and astrocytes but fails to infect human iPSC-derived microglia. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Th2P-4M presents a stronger neurotropism in hNSC-derived CNS cultures compared to Tha, suggesting that NF-κB- and JAK-STAT-mediated antiviral host responses define RABV replication and tropism. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: P- and M-proteins of Tha mediate evasion of NF-κB- and JAK-STAT-controlled antiviral host responses in neuronal cell types in contrast to glial cells. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes mechanistic roles of P- and M-proteins and evasion of NF-κB/JAK-STAT responses, but it does not make the specific comparative neurotropism claim about Th2P-4M vs Tha; thus the mechanistic portion is retained while the comparative phenotypic claim is not clearly supported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Astrocyte-like (SVGp12) and microglia-like (HMC3) cells protect neuroblastoma cells (SK-N-SH) from Tha infection in vitro. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tha-infected astrocytes and microglia protect neurons from Tha infection via a filtrable and transferable agent. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a similar protective effect of astrocytes and microglia on neurons and further characterizes it as mediated by a filtrable transferable agent; this clarifies the preprint's protection observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RABV tropism depends on its capability to evade cell-type specific immune responses via P- and M-proteins. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: P- and M-proteins of Tha mediate evasion of NF-κB- and JAK-STAT-controlled antiviral host responses in neuronal cell types in contrast to glial cells. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides an explicit mechanistic statement that aligns closely with the preprint's conclusion about P/M-mediated immune evasion determining tropism.

alignments.pub_claim_text: There are major differences in transcription profiles and quantification of intracellular protein levels between antiviral immune responses mediated by neurons, astrocytes, and microglia upon Tha infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This detailed transcriptional/proteomic comparison among cell types does not appear in the preprint and is presented newly in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides novel insights into RABV tropism, highlighting the importance of studying the interplay of CNS cell types during RABV infection. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This high-level contribution framing is present in the publication but was not explicitly stated as a claim in the preprint, so it is an added contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Slow EPSPs have a biphasic effect, initially increasing the likelihood of action potential generation by fast EPSPs, but later preventing it. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Slow EPSPs have a biphasic effect, likely suppressing fast EPSP evoked firing over very long periods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claim a biphasic effect; the publication emphasizes long-term suppression more strongly, clarifying the later-phase consequence while preserving the biphasic characterization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inhibitory effects of slow synaptic depolarizations on fast EPSP-induced action potentials were confirmed using live Ca imaging of enteric neurons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predictions were confirmed by Ca2+ imaging, which showed that stimuli evoking slow EPSPs suppressed activity evoked by fast EPSPs in many myenteric neurons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication directly confirms the imaging result described in the preprint with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Synchronous activation of GABA A receptors with fast EPSPs potentiates firing, while synchronous activation of GABA C receptors with fast EPSPs initially potentiates firing and then suppresses it. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Synchronous activation of GABAA receptors and fast EPSPs potentiates firing evoked by fast EPSPs, while synchronous activation of GABAC receptors with fast EPSPs first potentiates and then suppresses firing. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same pharmacological observation with matching wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results reveal a novel form of synaptic inhibition in the enteric nervous system that may account for prolonged quiescent periods in enteric motor patterns. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Slow EPSPs have a biphasic effect, likely suppressing fast EPSP evoked firing over very long periods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the prolonged suppression consequence of slow EPSPs, which supports and clarifies the preprint's conclusion that this mechanism could underlie prolonged quiescent motor periods, though the publication frames it more as a direct result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The quantity of information flowing across developing neural networks undergoes a dramatic increase across development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quantity of information flowing across developing neural networks undergoes a dramatic increase during development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording is effectively identical and the result is stated with the same strength in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The spatial structure of information flows is locked-in during early development and remains significantly temporally correlated over the first month of recording. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The spatial structure of information flows in developing neural networks tends to lock-in at the point when they arise. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication keeps the lock-in observation but softens timing/strength and omits the explicit claim about month-long temporal correlation, narrowing and tempering the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: During population bursts, nodes in the neural networks undertake specialised computational roles as transmitters, mediators, or receivers of information, which align with their spike ordering. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: During population bursts, nodes in the neural networks tend to undertake specialised computational roles as transmitters, mediators, or receivers of information. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the roles but removes the additional assertion about alignment with spike ordering, changing the content and narrowing scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The specialised computational roles occupied by nodes during bursts tend to be locked-in early in development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The specialised roles of nodes in information flow are regularly locked-in when the information flows are established. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases timing and weakens certainty ("tend" to "regularly" and lower certainty), clarifying and tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The phenomena of large increases in information flow and early lock-in of information flow spatial structure were also observed in a model network developing under an STDP learning rule. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Similar temporal patterns in the development of information flows were observed in a model network developing according to a spike-timing-dependent plasticity learning rule. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same modelling observation but with lower certainty and slightly different phrasing, softening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Drosophila melanogaster serves as a translational model for studying the genetic basis of psychostimulant drug consumption. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding publication claim explicitly asserts Drosophila as a translational model in the provided publication claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant genetic variation exists for voluntary consumption, preference, and changes in consumption and preference over time for cocaine and methamphetamine. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is significant genetic variation in voluntary consumption, preference, and change in consumption and preference over time for cocaine and methamphetamine. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim restates this result nearly verbatim with equal certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genome wide association analyses identified both shared and drug-specific candidate genes related to drug consumption. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genome wide association analyses identified both shared and drug-specific candidate genes that could be integrated into genetic interaction networks. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural circuitry underlying experience-dependent development of drug preference in Drosophila involves dopaminergic neurons and the mushroom bodies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dopaminergic neurons and the mushroom bodies are implicated as part of the neural circuitry underlying experience-dependent development of drug preference. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same circuit implication but states it with higher certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Ubiquitous RNA interference (RNAi) affected consumption behaviors for all 34 candidate genes assessed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result about RNAi effects on candidate genes is present in the publication claims but has no counterpart in the preprint claim list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two significant and partly replicable latent dimensions were identified linking cardiometabolic health and physical robustness to brain patterns of cortical thickness (CT) and grey matter volume (GMV). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified two significant and partly replicable latent dimensions linking cardiometabolic health and physical robustness to brain patterns of cortical thickness (CT) and gray matter volume (GMV). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this main result almost identically with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The association between the cardiometabolic health dimension and brain structural health was correlated to C-reactive protein (CRP), indicating a role for low-grade chronic inflammation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The association between the cardiometabolic health dimension and C-reactive protein (CRP) indicates a role for low-grade chronic inflammation in the relationship between cardiometabolic health and brain structural health. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the CRP link as indicating inflammation's role, clarifying and slightly elevating interpretive framing while keeping similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cardiometabolic health and physical robustness are associated with both increases and decreases in CT or GMV in some brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cardiometabolic health and physical robustness are associated with both increases and decreases of CT or GMV in some brain regions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the directional regional associations but reports lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings support the relevance of adopting a holistic perspective in health by integrating neurocognitive and physical health. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings contribute to the challenge to the classical conceptualization of neuropsychiatric and physical illnesses as categorical entities. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the interpretive implication from advocating a holistic perspective to more broadly challenging categorical disease conceptualizations, which is a substantive reframing with lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future studies should further examine the effects of risk/protective factors on different brain regions to deepen understanding of the clinical significance of changes in CT and GMV. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No explicit corresponding future-directions claim appears among the provided publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings demonstrate robust, multi-level and multivariate links between both cardiometabolic health and physical robustness with respect to CT, GMV, and neurotransmitter systems. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts broader multi-level links including neurotransmitter systems that are not present in the preprint claim list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpecXplore is an interactive python dashboard that facilitates mass spectral similarity matrix exploration. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpecXplore provides an interactive Python dashboard that facilitates mass spectral similarity matrix exploration. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpecXplore provides a two-dimensional t-SNE embedding for local connectivity exploration. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpecXplore utilizes state-of-the-art ms2deepscore pairwise spectral similarities as a quantitative backbone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpecXplore utilizes state-of-the-art ms2deepscore pairwise spectral similarities as a quantitative backbone. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same methodological claim with the same framing of ms2deepscore as the backbone.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SpecXplore can assist users in generating structural hypotheses for compounds of interest. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SpecXplore can assist users in the generation of structural hypotheses for compounds of interest. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this potential application with essentially identical wording and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: SpecXplore allows for fast changes of threshold and connectivity limitation settings, providing flexibility in data exploration. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This functionality is asserted in the publication claims but has no counterpart in the preprint claim list provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EnZymClass achieved high classification metric scores on the TE substrate specificity prediction task, with an average accuracy score of 0.8. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not report the specific numeric performance metric (accuracy 0.8); that explicit performance claim is absent from the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EnZymClass produced accuracy scores that are about twice as effective at avoiding misclassifications than existing similarity-based methods of substrate specificity prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance statement versus existing similarity-based methods is not present among the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By applying EnZymClass, two acyl-ACP TEs, ClFatB3 and CwFatB2, were identified with previously uncharacterized activity in E. coli fatty acid production hosts. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of EnZymClass led to the identification of two medium chain thioesterases, ClFatB3 and CwFatB2, with previously uncharacterized activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the identification result and states it with higher certainty; the substance is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Modifications incorporated into ClFatB3 resulted in a 4.2-fold overall improvement in observed C10 titers over the wildtype enzyme. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims provided do not include this specific experimental improvement metric for ClFatB3.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EnZymClass can be readily applied to other protein classification challenges. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EnZymClass can be applied to other protein classification challenges beyond thioesterases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the method's applicability to other protein classification problems with equivalent framing.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction of chain-length preference from primary sequence for plant acyl-ACP thioesterases has remained elusive until the development of EnZymClass. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts a broader novelty/contribution claim about solving a previously elusive prediction task that is not present in the preprint claim list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural activity in human early auditory cortical areas during speech perception exhibits a dual-scale spectral profile of power changes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pre-stimulus neural activity in early auditory cortical regions often exhibited power spectra with a shoulder in the delta range and a small bump in the beta range.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the dual-scale spectral characterization but specifies it in the pre-stimulus period and gives concrete spectral features (delta shoulder, beta bump), narrowing the temporal scope and adding detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Speech increases power in low (delta-theta) and high (gamma - high-gamma) frequency ranges, while decreasing power in intermediate (alpha-beta) frequencies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Speech decreased power in the beta range and increased power in the delta-theta and gamma ranges.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the pattern of increases and decreases but specifies beta as the decreased intermediate band, making the frequency characterization more precise.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single-trial multivariate decoding resulted in a bimodal spectral profile of information content, with better decoding at low and high frequencies than at intermediate ones.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We obtained better phase than power information decoding, and a bimodal spectral profile of information content with better decoding at low (delta-theta) and high (gamma) frequencies than at intermediate (beta) frequencies.
alignments.rationale: The core bimodal decoding result is preserved; the publication adds the detail that phase decoding outperformed power decoding and specifies the intermediate band as beta.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The patterns observed in spectral and informational perspectives are consistent with the activity of a relatively simple computational model comprising two reciprocally connected excitatory/inhibitory sub-networks operating at different timescales.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Modeling and experimental results were similar in terms of pre-stimulus spectral profile, spectral modulations with speech, and spectral profile of information content.
alignments.rationale: Both claims link modeling and data, but the publication frames the correspondence more generally (similarity of modeling and experimental results) without restating the detailed E/I subnetwork architecture, thus clarifying the nature of the model-data match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We provide consistent evidence for the existence, information coding value and underlying neuronal architecture of dual timescale processing in human auditory cortex.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We provide converging evidence for a dual timescale processing infrastructure in human auditory cortex, consistent with the dynamics of a simple rate model.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains evidence for dual timescale processing but frames it as consistent with a simple rate model rather than asserting a specific underlying neuronal architecture, making the claim more cautious and narrower on mechanistic detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More than 500 records of IAV infections in mice whose viral proteins could be retrieved were documented.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the documented dataset size or retrieval of viral proteins; this descriptive dataset claim is omitted in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PART models learned from full or subsets of datasets achieved the best performance, with moderate averaged model accuracies ranging from 65.0% to 84.4% for two-class datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PART models achieved the best performance, with moderate average model accuracies ranging from 65.0 to 84.4% for the two-class problem.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance range and the superior performance of PART models without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models based on a dataset that included all IAV strains achieved a better averaged accuracy when host information was taken into account.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average accuracy of the models was improved when host information was taken into account.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result that including host information improved average model accuracy; the dataset scope is implicit but not contradicted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several sites with a known contribution to virulence were found as the top protein sites, and site pairs that may synergistically influence virulence were also uncovered.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several sites with a known contribution to virulence were found as the top protein sites.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the identification of known virulence sites but omits mention of synergistic site pairs, narrowing the claim to single-site findings.
