alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 3D U-Net achieved a Dice score of 0.92±0.06 for organoid segmentation in the T2*-w sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel non-invasive approach for monitoring cerebral organoids demonstrates performance on par or better than state-of-the-art tools applied to brightfield imaging. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Compactness separated low- and high-quality organoids with high accuracy, achieving a ROC AUC of 0.98. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Organoid growth can be monitored reliably over time and cystic and non-cystic organoids can be separated with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DTI showed that low-quality organoids have a significantly higher diffusion than high-quality organoids (p < .001). (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel non-invasive approach to monitor and analyze cerebral organoids over time using high-field MRI and automated image analysis tools is presented. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel non-invasive approach for monitoring cerebral organoids demonstrates performance on par or better than state-of-the-art tools applied to brightfield imaging. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Local cyst segmentation is feasible but could be further improved in the future. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Cerebral organoids recapitulate the structure and function of the developing human brain in vitro. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Global signal regression (GSR) has negligible to small effects on connectivity within small resting state networks (RSNs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Global signal regression (GSR) has negligible to small effects on effective connectivity within small resting state networks (RSNs). (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Data without GSR are more informative than data with GSR, although GSR improves the precision of posterior estimates in small resting state networks. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: GSR is a minor concern in dynamic causal modeling (DCM) studies, but caution is needed in the quantitative interpretation of between-network connections and noise parameters. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Correlational tractography found networks of fiber tracts that were either positively or negatively correlated with lesion status throughout the brain. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results suggest that correlational tractography is a reasonable tool to examine whole brain structural changes in rodent models of neurodegenerative disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Correlational tractography is a reasonable tool to examine whole brain structural changes in rodent models of neurodegenerative disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A highly conserved immune-related module was identified across all models and patient cohorts of Alzheimer’s disease and epilepsy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A highly conserved immune-related module was identified across all models and patient cohorts of Alzheimer's disease and epilepsy. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: A properly normalized heuristic MAX observer (SNN-MAX) approximates optimal performance in detecting an additive wavelet target in white noise. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The pattern of hits and correct rejections for the ideal and SNN-MAX observers aligns with human performance data. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different marmoset calls elicit distinct activation patterns in the brain. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study indicates the involvement of the cerebellum and medial prefrontal cortex in distinguishing particular vocalizations from others. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatial encoding persisted in the N-zone even when non-navigational behaviors were excluded. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of distance estimation decreased as the animal approached the E-zone, correlating with dynamic decision-making related to food procurement and predator evasion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of distance estimation decreased as the animal approached the E-zone, where neuronal activity was more correlated with dynamic decision-making. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: A naive Bayesian classifier was able to predict the success and failure of avoidance behavior up to 6 seconds before head withdrawal. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: An overlapping population of mPFC neurons may switch between encoding for goal-directed navigation and imminent situational challenges. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A simple linear Sp-AAD model is susceptible to overfitting to confounding signal patterns such as eye-gaze and trial fingerprints. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A simple linear Sp-AAD model is susceptible to overfitting to confounding signal patterns such as eye-gaze and trial fingerprints, resulting in artificially high decoding accuracies. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Regional variation in receptor density explains variations in synaptic connectivity and ensuing cortical population dynamics.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same idea but more explicitly ties receptor distributions to synaptic connectivity and resulting population dynamics, adding mechanistic detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The output of this modelling provides a cortical atlas of neurobiologically informed intracortical synaptic connectivity parameters.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The output is a cortical atlas of neurobiologically informed intracortical synaptic connectivity parameters.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the atlas contribution and presents it with stronger (published) certainty/positioning (higher reported certainty).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work can help to explain regional variations in human electrophysiology and may provide a methodological foundation to integrate multi-modal data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work helps to explain regional variations in human electrophysiology.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the explanatory claim about regional electrophysiology but omits the more speculative methodological-foundation statement, narrowing the claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Dynamic causal models generated region-specific intracranial EEG spectra accurately.
alignments.rationale: This specific statement about dynamic causal models producing accurate region-specific iEEG spectra does not have a direct counterpart in the preprint and appears as an added published claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We achieved on average 55% accuracy using only 6 channels of data recorded with a single minimally invasive sEEG electrode in the first patient.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We achieved on average 55% accuracy using only six channels of data recorded with a single minimally invasive sEEG electrode in the first patient.
alignments.rationale: The result is reproduced verbatim (minor numeric/format equivalence only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We achieved 70% accuracy using only 8 channels of data recorded for a single ECoG strip in the second patient in classifying 26+1 overtly pronounced words.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We achieved 70% accuracy using only eight channels of data recorded for a single ECoG strip in the second patient in classifying 26+1 overtly pronounced words.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this performance claim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our compact architecture did not require the use of pre-engineered features, learned fast and resulted in a stable, interpretable and physiologically meaningful decision rule.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our compact architecture did not require the use of pre-engineered features, learned fast and resulted in a stable, interpretable and physiologically meaningful decision rule.
alignments.rationale: The methodological/conclusion claim is preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spatial characteristics of the pivotal neuronal populations corroborate with active and passive speech mapping results and exhibit the inverse space-frequency relationship characteristic of neural activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatial characteristics of the pivotal neuronal populations corroborate with active and passive speech mapping results and exhibit the inverse space-frequency relationship characteristic of neural activity.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this conclusion without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our compact solution performed on par or better than those recently featured in neural speech decoding literature.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our compact solution performed on par or better than those recently featured in neural speech decoding literature.
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance claim remains the same in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gene expression-based machine learning classifier identified samples with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) mutational signature at an AUROC of 0.94 in the MyBrCa validation dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The gene expression-based machine learning classifier identified samples with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) mutational signature at an AUROC of 0.93 in MyBrCa validation datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a very similar result but with a slightly lower AUROC value and pluralized/expanded dataset wording, indicating a small numerical revision and modest tempering.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier strongly segregated HRD-associated genomic features in triple-negative breast cancers (TNBCs) from TCGA and METABRIC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifier strongly segregated HRD-associated genomic features in TNBCs from TCGA, METABRIC, and ICGC.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the datasets to include ICGC in addition to TCGA and METABRIC, broadening the scope of the segregation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The RNA-seq results correlated strongly with NanoString results from fresh frozen tissue (r = 0.90) and from FFPE tissue (r = 0.84).
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the stated correlation coefficients or the explicit NanoString correlation result, so this specific quantitative claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gene expression classifier may identify triple-negative breast cancer patients with homologous recombination deficiency, suggesting an alternative method to identify individuals who may benefit from treatment with PARP inhibitors or platinum chemotherapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The gene expression classifier may identify triple-negative breast cancer patients with homologous recombination deficiency.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the identification claim but omits the explicit treatment-implication wording in this specific matched claim (that implication appears as a separate claim below).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier suggests an alternative method to identify individuals who may benefit from treatment with PARP inhibitors or platinum chemotherapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifier suggests an alternative method to identify individuals who may benefit from treatment with PARP inhibitors or platinum chemotherapy.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this clinical-implication claim verbatim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifier demonstrated an AUROC of 0.84 in TCGA TNBCs.
alignments.rationale: This specific numeric performance result on TCGA TNBCs (AUROC=0.84) does not appear in the preprint claim list and is introduced in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 1D-CNN based model performed best compared to other models for each set of features.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention a 1D‑CNN as best; the claim about 1D‑CNN performance is absent in the published claims, so it appears removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model developed using amino acid binary profile achieved an AUROC of 0.83 for the training dataset and 0.74 for the validation dataset.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report these amino acid binary profile training/validation AUROC numbers or a matching claim, so this specific result was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model generated using PSSM profile resulted in an AUROC of 0.91 for the training dataset and 0.74 for the validation dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The eXtreme Gradient Boosting-based model achieved a maximum area under the receiver operating characteristics (AUROC) curve of 0.77 on the independent dataset using PSSM profile.
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern PSSM-based performance, but the publication reports a different model, a lower AUROC on an independent dataset (0.77) rather than the preprint's training/validation figures, indicating a revision and tempering of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DBPred is an effective approach to predict the DNA interacting residues in the protein using its primary structure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method outperformed all existing methods when evaluated on the independent dataset.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general effectiveness claim is made more specific in the publication (outperforming all existing methods on an independent dataset), which clarifies and strengthens the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hybrid model, which combines all features, performed best with an AUROC of 0.91 for the training dataset and 0.79 for the validation dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning-based model achieved the highest AUROC of 0.79 on the independent dataset using a combination of all three profiles.
alignments.rationale: Both refer to the combined/hybrid model, but the publication reports a single independent‑dataset AUROC (0.79) and frames it as a deep learning model; training/validation numbers changed, so the result was revised and tempered.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Standalone software and a web server were developed to facilitate access for the scientific community.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an implementation/contribution (software and web server) that was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multi-run CAE (mrCAE) identifies a stable set of features that can effectively distinguish between 12 different types of cancers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multi-run CAE (mrCAE) performs better in feature selection than single-run CAE and other state-of-the-art feature selection techniques.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's claim about identifying a stable discriminative feature set is captured in the publication's emphasis on superior feature selection performance; phrasing is clarified toward comparative performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: mrCAE outperforms single-run CAE, standard autoencoder (AE), and other state-of-the-art feature selection techniques in feature selection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multi-run CAE (mrCAE) performs better in feature selection than single-run CAE and other state-of-the-art feature selection techniques.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this comparative performance claim essentially unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study discovered a set of 128 lncRNAs that can identify the origin of 12 different cancers with an accuracy of 95%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A set of top-ranking 128 lncRNAs was identified that could accurately identify the origin of 12 different cancers with an accuracy of 95%.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific result (128 lncRNAs, 95% accuracy) with essentially identical wording and metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Survival analysis indicated that 76 of the 128 lncRNAs have prognostic capability in differentiating high- and low-risk groups of patients across different cancers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 76 of the 128 identified lncRNAs have the prognostic capability to differentiate high- and low-risk groups of patients with different cancers.
alignments.rationale: The prognostic finding is directly preserved in the publication with the same counts and interpretation.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed mrCAE can be valuable for precision medicine by selecting actual features instead of pseudo-features.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an added interpretive/concluding statement about potential value for precision medicine that was not stated in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Orca predicts genome 3D architecture from kilobase to whole-chromosome scale, covering structures including chromatin compartments and topologically associating domains.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orca is a sequence-based deep-learning approach that predicts 3D genome architecture from kilobase to whole-chromosome scale.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates Orca's core capability (sequence-based prediction from kilobase to chromosome scale) consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models accurately recapitulate effects of experimentally studied structural variants at varying sizes (300bp-80Mb) using only sequence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orca recapitulated effects of experimentally studied variants at varying sizes from 300 bp to 90 Mb.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe recapitulation of variant effects, but the publication reports a slightly different upper size (90 Mb vs 80 Mb) and frames it in the published evaluation, so the quantitative detail was revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At the compartment scale, sequences at active transcription start sites are primarily responsible for establishing the expression-active compartment A.
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the compartment scale, virtual screens of sequence activities suggest a model for the sequence basis of chromatin compartments.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts a specific mechanism (TSS sequences primarily responsible); the publication tones this into a suggested model from virtual screens, which is a revision and a tempering of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inactive compartment B typically requires extended stretches of AT-rich sequences (at least 6-12kb) and can form ‘passively’ without depending on any particular sequence pattern.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Orca captures the sequence dependencies of structures including chromatin compartments and topologically associating domains.
alignments.rationale: The preprint makes a specific mechanistic claim about AT-rich stretches for compartment B, whereas the publication gives a more general statement about capturing sequence dependencies; the specific mechanistic assertion appears softened or subsumed into a broader claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Orca provides an in silico model to help study the sequence-dependent mechanistic basis of genome architecture.
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the compartment scale, virtual screens of sequence activities suggest a model for the sequence basis of chromatin compartments.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents virtual screens and a suggested model for sequence basis of compartments, which clarifies and operationalizes the preprint's statement about providing an in silico model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: IPTDFold is significantly superior to the distance-assisted fragment assembly method Rosetta_D.
alignments.pub_claim_text: IPTDFold is significantly superior to the distance-assisted fragment assembly method Rosetta_D.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this comparative performance claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of IPTDFold does not decrease as the length of the protein increases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of IPTDFold does not decrease as the length of the protein increases.
alignments.rationale: The claim about length robustness is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When using the same FastRelax protocol, the prediction accuracy of IPTDFold is significantly superior to that of trRosetta without orientation constraints.
alignments.pub_claim_text: When using the same FastRelax protocol, the prediction accuracy of IPTDFold is significantly superior to that of trRosetta without orientation constraints.
alignments.rationale: This methodological comparison is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of IPTDFold is equivalent to that of the full version of trRosetta.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of IPTDFold is equivalent to that of the full version of trRosetta.
alignments.rationale: The content of the claim is preserved, but the publication lists a reduced certainty (medium) versus the preprint's high certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method for brain extraction outperforms two commonly used and publicly available methods on clinical quality images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method for brain extraction outperforms two commonly used and publicly available methods on clinical quality images.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the brain extraction performance claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identify a decrease in total brain volume of -2.4cc/year in the large-scale clinical multi-site MRI-GENIE study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identify a decrease in total brain volume of -2.4 cc/year in the large-scale clinical multi-site MRI-GENIE study.
alignments.rationale: The reported annual brain volume decrease is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resulting total brain volumes can successfully be used for quality control of image preprocessing.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present this specific QC-usage claim about the derived total brain volumes, suggesting it was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate the increase of WMH burden with age at a rate of 0.950 cc/year.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate the increase of WMH burden with age at a rate of 0.950 cc/year.
alignments.rationale: The WMH burden increase rate is preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single site estimates of WMH burden can be biased by the number of subjects recruited.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single site estimates of WMH burden can be biased by the number of subjects recruited.
alignments.rationale: The limitation regarding single-site estimate bias is maintained in the publication, though labeled as a conclusion there; the substantive content is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed microscale computational model of an epileptic neocortical column allows for the simulation of realistic interictal epileptic signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed microscale computational model of an epileptic neocortical column can simulate realistic interictal epileptic signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and meaning are effectively identical between the preprint and publication; both assert the model simulates realistic interictal signals with high certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model was validated through comparisons with real interictal epileptiform events recorded using intracranial stereo-electroencephalography signals from both humans and animals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to an explicit validation-comparison statement involving intracranial stereo-EEG from humans and animals; the claim appears omitted in the published list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model enables the exploration of the relationship between glutamatergic and GABAergic synaptic transmission and the type of generated interictal epileptiform events. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model reveals the relationship between glutamatergic and GABAergic synaptic transmission and the type of generated Interictal Epileptiform Events (IEEs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the preprint's exploratory phrasing to a definitive 'reveals' result with higher certainty, indicating a substantive amplification of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The modeling framework provides an Electrode Tissue Model that adds realism to the simulated signals and allows for the study of their sensitivity to electrode characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The modeling framework provides an Electrode Tissue Model (ETI) that enhances the realism of simulated signals and allows for the study of their sensitivity to electrode characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same Electrode Tissue Model contribution and its purpose with essentially unchanged content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NeoCoMM model can be utilized for sensitivity analysis and hypothesis testing in various applications related to epilepsy research. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NeoCoMM model can be utilized for sensitivity analysis and hypothesis testing in various applications related to epilepsy research. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim in preprint and publication about NeoCoMM's use for sensitivity analysis and hypothesis testing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep networks model the human contrast sensitivity function (CSF) faithfully, making them suitable candidates for applications of image quality and compression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep networks model the human contrast sensitivity function (CSF) faithfully, making them suitable candidates for applications of image quality and compression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and certainty are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The exact shape of the networks’ CSF appears to be task-dependent. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The exact shape of the networks' CSF appears to be task-dependent. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in content and certainty between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Human-like CSF emerges in mid- and high-level tasks such as edge detection and object recognition. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human-like CSF emerges in mid- and high-level tasks such as edge detection and object recognition. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim across preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Visual representation from all levels of visual hierarchy contributes to the tuning curve of the CSF. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Visual representation from all levels of visual hierarchy contributes to the tuning curve of the CSF. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No substantive change between preprint and published claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach highlights the importance of accounting for both temporal and spatial dynamism in whole brain neuroimaging data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach highlights the importance of accounting for both temporal and spatial dynamism in whole brain neuroimaging data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about accounting for temporal and spatial dynamism is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our analysis reveals widespread group level differences across multiple networks and spatiotemporal features in individuals with schizophrenia compared to controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our analysis reveals widespread group level differences across multiple networks and spatiotemporal features in individuals with schizophrenia compared to controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result phrasing and certainty are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep residual convolutional neural network model can extract 53 different spatiotemporal networks that capture dynamism within various domains related to schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep residual convolutional neural network model can extract 53 different spatiotemporal networks that capture dynamism within various domains. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same extraction result but with higher certainty and without the explicit 'related to schizophrenia' wording, effectively clarifying and increasing confidence in the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Highly structured and neuroanatomically relevant results are missed if one does not consider the voxel-wise spatial dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Highly structured and neuroanatomically relevant results are missed if one does not consider the voxel-wise spatial dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation statement is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Laughter induced widespread activity in ventral visual cortex and superior and middle temporal and motor cortices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Laughter induces widespread activity in ventral visual cortex and superior and middle temporal and motor cortices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claims are effectively identical in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Crying activated thalamus, cingulate cortex along the anterior-posterior axis, insula and orbitofrontal cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Crying activates the thalamus, cingulate cortex along the anterior-posterior axis, insula, and orbitofrontal cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in anatomical activation claims between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both laughter and crying could be decoded accurately (66-77% depending on the experiment) from the BOLD signal. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both laughter and crying could be decoded accurately (66-77% depending on the experiment) from the BOLD signal. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Decoding accuracy claim preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distinct neural networks are engaged in processing laughter and crying signals. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Perceiving laughter and crying engage distinct neural networks, whose activity suppresses each other to manage appropriate behavioral responses to others' bonding and distress signals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the core claim of distinct networks but adds an interaction mechanism (mutual suppression) and links to behavioral responses; overall certainty is lower in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Coordinated activity of these networks allows managing appropriate behavioral responses to others’ bonding and distress signals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Perceiving laughter and crying engage distinct neural networks, whose activity suppresses each other to manage appropriate behavioral responses to others' bonding and distress signals. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication merges the coordinated-activity conclusion with an explicit suppression mechanism; the behavioral-response conclusion is preserved though phrased within a broader mechanistic statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The subtype selectivity of designed selective ligands can be explained by ligand binding to the conformationally distinct states between CB 1 and CB 2. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The subtype selectivity of designed selective ligands can be explained by the ligand binding to the conformationally distinct states between cannabinoid receptors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same mechanistic conclusion about subtype selectivity via binding to conformationally distinct receptor states; wording generalized to 'cannabinoid receptors' but meaning unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Toggle switch residue movement and its effect on receptor activation differ greatly between CB 1 and CB 2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of ~700 μs of unbiased simulations identifies the similarities and distinctions between the activation mechanism of CB1 and CB2 receptors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a broader analysis of activation similarities and distinctions (including toggle-switch effects) rather than the preprint's specific focus on a toggle-switch residue, thus broadening and slightly reframing the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differential binding pocket volume leads to distinct binding poses and docking affinities of CB 2 selective agonists in CB 1. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Structural and dynamic comparisons of metastable intermediate states reveal a distinction in the binding pocket volume change during CB1 and CB2 activation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication emphasizes the binding-pocket volume distinction as revealed by structural/dynamic comparisons, which aligns with and clarifies the preprint's pocket-volume explanation for differing binding poses/affinities.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only a few of the intermediate metastable states of CB 1 show high affinity towards CB 2 selective agonists. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Docking analysis shows that only a few of the intermediate metastable states of CB1 exhibit high affinity towards CB2 selective agonists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the specific docking-affinity result but presents it with higher certainty (amplified) and explicit docking analysis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This computational study mechanistically explains the subtype selectivity of CB 2 selective ligands by deciphering the activation mechanism of cannabinoid receptors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The subtype selectivity of designed selective ligands can be explained by the ligand binding to the conformationally distinct states between cannabinoid receptors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the study's overarching mechanistic explanation for subtype selectivity in equivalent terms.

alignments.pub_claim_text: All the CB2 metastable states show a similar affinity for CB2 selective agonists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific result (uniform CB2 metastable-state affinity for CB2 agonists) does not appear among the preprint claims and is therefore an added publication-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mutual information (MI) analysis is a valid technique for studying neural envelope tracking. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mutual information (MI) analysis detects nonlinear relations in neural envelope tracking, proving its added value over linear models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames MI's validity in terms of detecting nonlinear relations and added value over linear models, a more specific formulation of the preprint's general validity claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Gaussian copula approach provides the most reliable and robust results for MI analysis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Gaussian copula approach provides the most reliable and robust results for MI analysis in studying neural envelope tracking. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same specific claim about the Gaussian copula approach with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MI analysis can detect nonlinear components on the single-subject level, which linear models cannot. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Nonlinear components were robustly detected in the neural response to the speech envelope on the single-subject level using MI analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same single-subject detection claim using MI analysis with comparable strength and wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MI analysis is a statistically more powerful tool for studying neural envelope tracking than linear models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mutual information (MI) analysis detects nonlinear relations in neural envelope tracking, proving its added value over linear models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes added value (detecting nonlinear relations) rather than explicitly claiming greater statistical power, a shift in emphasis that softens the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using traditional MI methodologies can introduce bias that severely impacts interpretations of results. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or highlight the preprint's limitation about severe bias from traditional MI methods, so this limitation appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neuropeptide-elicited intrinsic bursting can switch a neuron from single to dual-network participation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gly<sup>1</sup>-SIFamide increased LPG intrinsic excitability and rebound from inhibition and decreased spike frequency adaptation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports specific neuropeptide effects on intrinsic properties that provide a mechanistic specification of the preprint's claim about peptide-elicited switching.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiple intrinsic properties are modulated in the dual-network state, contributing to neuronal switching. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A switch from single- to dual-network activity can involve modulation of multiple intrinsic properties. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core conclusion that multiple intrinsic properties are modulated during switching, with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bursting at the second network frequency is more sensitive to blocking currents in the dual-network state than when neurons are synaptically isolated from their home network. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bursting at the second network frequency was more sensitive to blocking currents in the dual-network state than when neurons were synaptically isolated from their home network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same result but with higher certainty (medium -> high), so the claim content is unchanged while confidence is increased.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Synaptic input can shape the contributions of modulated intrinsic properties underlying dual-network activity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Synaptic input can shape the contributions of modulated intrinsic properties underlying dual-network activity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains an equivalent conclusion about synaptic input shaping intrinsic property contributions with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed computational approaches significantly outperform other competing methods on three classification tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our proposed computational approaches significantly outperform other competing methods on three classification tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance claim with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Chromatin features are major contributors to the prediction of cell-type-specific and shared binding sites, with chromatin accessibility being the best predictor. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified independent feature contributions for cell-type-specific and shared sites through SHAP values. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports identification of feature contributions via SHAP values but does not assert the strong claim that chromatin accessibility is the single best predictor, reducing specificity and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN-based model's prediction performance is significantly improved by including chromatin accessibility. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN-based model can predict cell-type-specific and shared binding sites by excluding or including DNase signals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication indicates the model can work with or without DNase (chromatin accessibility) signals, which contradicts the preprint's stronger claim that including accessibility significantly improves performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Independent feature contributions for cell-type-specific and shared sites were identified, with chromatin features being predominant in cell-type-specific sites and motif features in shared sites. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified independent feature contributions for cell-type-specific and shared sites through SHAP values. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms that independent contributions were identified via SHAP but omits the specific partitioning (chromatin vs motif predominance) stated in the preprint, reducing the claim's specificity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Local field potentials in the avian equivalent of the prefrontal cortex are modulated by working memory. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Local field potentials in the avian equivalent of the prefrontal cortex are modulated by working memory. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this core experimental result with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Narrow gamma (30-59 Hz) and beta band (13-19 Hz) frequencies contain information about the location of target items and are influenced by working memory load. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Narrow gamma and beta band frequencies contain information about the location of target items and are modulated by working memory load. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same frequency-band-specific information and modulation claims with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The avian associative pallium can create local field potential signatures similar to those observed in primates, despite lacking cortical organization. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The avian associative pallium can create local field potential signatures reminiscent of those observed in primates despite lacking a cortical organization. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conclusion about LFP signatures in the avian pallium relative to primates with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Crows exhibit bursts in beta and gamma frequencies that are similar to those observed in monkeys, supporting 'activity silent' models of working memory. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly report the claim linking bursts to supporting 'activity silent' working memory models, so this specific interpretive claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural networks required for local field potential phenomena have evolved in parallel in birds and mammals, indicating a critical cognitive function of oscillatory dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Oscillatory dynamics have evolved through convergence in species capable of complex cognition. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes parallel evolution as convergent evolution in cognitively complex species, a close conceptual restatement with slightly different wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method of significant subgraph mining can be employed as a useful tool in network comparison. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method of significant subgraph mining can be employed as a useful tool in neural network comparison. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the domain qualifier 'neural' to the general method claim, clarifying its intended application without changing core meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An extension of the method to dependent graph generating processes is provided, applicable in within-subject experimental designs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An extension of the method to dependent graph generating processes is applicable in within-subject experimental designs. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same extension and applicability but reports it with lower stated certainty, so content is unchanged while confidence is reduced.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An empirical power analysis suggests that the appropriate sample size for studies comparing autism spectrum patients with neurotypical controls should be n=60 per group or larger. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports an empirical power analysis in a different framing (transfer entropy networks and group differences) but does not present the specific sample-size recommendation, so the exact claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prenatal alcohol exposure is associated with altered neural reading networks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prenatal alcohol exposure is linked to atypical brain and cognitive development, including poor academic performance in reading.
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens the preprint's specific claim about neural reading networks to a more general statement about atypical brain and cognitive development that includes reading; the confidence level remains high in both.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FASD subtypes exhibit distinctive neural correlates of phonological processing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distinctive neural correlates of reading (dis)abilities were observed between adolescents with and without facial dysmorphology.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same distinction (with/without facial dysmorphology) in terms of reading-related neural correlates, clarifying the comparison while preserving the original claim's meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater right-hemispheric reliance was observed in FASD with facial dysmorphology.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Syndromal adolescents showed greater activation in the right precentral gyrus during phonological processing compared to the HE and control groups.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the right-hemisphere effect anatomically (right precentral gyrus) and in the phonological task, which clarifies and details the preprint's result without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Non-syndromal FASD showed deficits in the typical left-hemispheric reading network.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The brain function-behavior relation in the HE group was weaker than among the controls, suggesting less efficient function of the typical reading network.
alignments.rationale: The publication couches the preprint's deficit claim as a weaker brain function-behavior relationship (medium certainty), which is a more cautious formulation, hence a clarification with reduced confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: In the HE group, better reading skills were positively correlated with neural activation in the left angular gyrus and white matter organization of the left ILF.
alignments.rationale: This specific brain–behavior correlation in the HE group is reported in the publication but has no direct counterpart in the preprint, so it is an added result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The existence of a locomotor-to-ocular motor coupling in the mouse is demonstrated for the first time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates for the first time the existence of a locomotor-to-ocular motor coupling in the mouse.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint's primary novel result verbatim in scope and tone.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spino-extraocular motor coupling is evidenced from newborn mice ex vivo preparations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In neonates, ex vivo nerve recordings reveal a spino-extraocular motor coupled activity similar to that described in Xenopus.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same neonatal ex vivo evidence of spino-extraocular coupling, with the publication explicitly comparing it to Xenopus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adult decerebrated mice exhibit conjugated rhythmic eye movements during treadmill locomotion.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Treadmill-elicited locomotion in decerebrated preparations evokes rhythmic eye movements in synchrony with the limb gait pattern.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result but adds that the eye movements are synchronous with limb gait, a clarifying detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Locomotor-induced oculomotor activity occurs in the absence of visuo-vestibular inputs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Treadmill-elicited locomotion in decerebrated preparations evokes rhythmic eye movements in synchrony with the limb gait pattern.
alignments.rationale: The publication's decerebrated preparation implies reduced visuo-vestibular contributions and reports locomotor-driven eye movements; the publication does not explicitly state the absence of visuo-vestibular inputs, so this is a partial clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a conservation of the spino-extraocular coupling in higher tetrapods during sustained locomotion.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data provide evidence for the conservation of locomotor-induced eye movements in vertebrate lineages.
alignments.rationale: The publication draws a similar conclusion about conservation but frames it more broadly across vertebrate lineages rather than only higher tetrapods, slightly broadening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sensory deprivation due to blindness results in intra- and cross-modal plasticity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sensory deprivation due to blindness results in compensatory plasticity in the brain.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's core conclusion about plasticity due to blindness; wording shifts (intra-/cross-modal vs compensatory) but the meaning is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Congenital cataract-reversal individuals exhibit a larger increase in the amplitude of slow BOLD fluctuations in visual cortex during rest with eyes open compared to normally sighted controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Congenital cataract-reversal individuals exhibit a larger increase in amplitude of low-frequency fluctuations in visual cortex compared to normally sighted controls when their eyes are open.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint's result with equivalent technical phrasing (low-frequency vs slow BOLD fluctuations) and identical conditions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Congenitally blind individuals show a higher amplitude in slow BOLD fluctuations in visual cortex compared to sighted individuals and congenital cataract-reversal individuals in the eyes closed condition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Congenitally blind individuals show a higher amplitude of low-frequency fluctuations in visual cortex compared to both sighted individuals and congenital cataract-reversal individuals in the eyes closed condition.
alignments.rationale: This result is carried over verbatim in substance and scope between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lower parietal increase and the missing downregulation in auditory and sensorimotor regions in congenital cataract-reversal individuals suggest a reduced influence of the visual system on multisensory and remaining sensory systems after restoring sight.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lower parietal increase and the missing downregulation in auditory regions in congenital cataract-reversal individuals suggest a reduced influence of the visual system on multisensory and other sensory systems after restoring sight.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the main inference but omits mention of sensorimotor regions, narrowing the anatomical scope while keeping the interpretive conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results demonstrate a crucial dependence of multisensory neural networks on visual experience during a sensitive phase in human brain development.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These results demonstrate a crucial dependence of visual and multisensory neural system functioning on visual experience during a sensitive phase in human brain development.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's developmental-dependence conclusion with slightly different phrasing that emphasizes both visual and multisensory system functioning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sparsely synchronized rhythms (SSRs) enhance pattern separation performed in the hippocampal dentate gyrus (DG).
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts that SSRs enhance pattern separation; the publication focuses on SSR dynamics and firing properties without making this systems-level enhancement claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: During winner-take-all competition, SSRs appear in each population of granule cells (GCs) and basket cells (BCs) due to the interaction of excitation of the GCs with inhibition of the BCs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sparsely synchronized rhythms (SSRs) appear in each population of granule cells (GCs) and basket cells (BCs) through the interaction of excitation of the GCs with inhibition of the BCs.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the mechanism for SSR emergence identically to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individual granule cells exhibit random spike skipping, leading to a multi-peaked inter-spike-interval histogram.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individual granule cells exhibit random spike skipping, leading to a multi-peaked inter-spike-interval histogram.
alignments.rationale: This specific single-cell firing property is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both basket cells and mossy cells show 'intrastripe' burstings within stripes, along with 'interstripe' random spike skipping.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both basket cells and mossy cells show 'intrastripe' burstings within stripes, along with random spike skipping.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same combined bursting and skipping phenomena in BCs and MCs, matching the preprint's description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Changes in the population and individual firings in the SSRs occur with decreasing fractions of mossy cells, which may lead to MC loss during epileptogenesis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Changes in the population and individual firings in the SSRs occur with decreasing the fraction of mossy cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the effect of decreasing mossy cell fraction on SSR firing but omits the speculative link to epileptogenesis, narrowing the claim's scope.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The population-averaged mean-firing-rate of granule cells is less than the population frequency.
alignments.rationale: This specific population-level firing-rate observation appears in the publication but is not stated in the preprint, so it is an added result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Minimizing a wiring cost on spatially organized feedforward and lateral connections within IT results in a hierarchical, topographic organization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Minimizing a wiring cost on spatially organized feedforward and lateral connections results in a hierarchical, topographic organization in the IT cortex.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the preprint's modeling result with equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Interactive Topographic Network framework demonstrates that the spatial clustering of domains in the primate visual system may arise from the demands of visual recognition under the constraints of minimal wiring costs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Topographic domain selectivity is an emergent property of a visual system optimized to maximize behavioral performance under generic connectivity-based constraints.
alignments.rationale: Both statements express the same mechanistic conclusion that domain-selectivity emerges from optimization for behavioral performance under wiring/connectivity constraints.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The learned organization of the model captures many properties of organization in primates, including domain-selective spatial clusters and columnar responses across separate excitatory and inhibitory units.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hierarchical, topographic organization replicates key properties of primate IT cortex, including domain-selective spatial clusters for faces, objects, and scenes.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes replication of key primate IT properties (naming specific domains) which clarifies and specifies the preprint's broader statement about captured properties.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Domain-selectivity is an emergent property of a visual system optimized to maximize behavioral performance while minimizing wiring costs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Topographic domain selectivity is an emergent property of a visual system optimized to maximize behavioral performance under generic connectivity-based constraints.
alignments.rationale: The publication's conclusion matches the preprint's claim about emergent domain-selectivity from optimization under connectivity/wiring constraints.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Simon effect SSM (SE-SSM) captures the behavioral data in spatial conflict tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Simon effect SSM (SE-SSM) captures the behavioral data in spatial conflict tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text of P0 and Q0 is effectively identical, so the claim is unchanged in semantics, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two conflict specific model parameters of the SE-SSM have clear across subject BOLD correlates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two conflict specific model parameters of the SE-SSM have clear across subject BOLD correlates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: P1 and Q1 are the same result claim about BOLD correlates; no substantive change is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Examining BOLD in terms of the expected decision-variable leads to a specific pattern of activation that would not be otherwise possible to extract. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Examining BOLD in terms of the expected decision-variable leads to a specific pattern of activation that would not be otherwise possible to extract. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about BOLD and expected decision-variable is reproduced verbatim in the publication, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model selection and model validation should account for both residual errors and model complexity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model selection and model validation should account for both residual errors and model complexity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation about accounting for residuals and complexity is the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Many studies in mathematical biology do not consider model validation or model selection. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The specific critical observation about the prevalence of omitted validation/selection is not present in the publication's listed claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neglecting the role of model complexity can give rise to misleading outcomes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neglecting the role of model complexity can give rise to misleading outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is the same but the publication expresses it with reduced certainty (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results provide a clear methodology that can be used to guide model selection across a range of applications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide a straightforward methodology that can be used to guide model selection across a range of applications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication rewords 'clear' to 'straightforward' without changing the substantive claim; this is a minor clarification in phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our experience of time is directly and intrinsically computed within the motor system. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not endorse the strong, direct formulation that 'experience of time is directly and intrinsically computed' in the motor system; instead it adopts a more tentative/qualified formulation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subjects in the free-moving group demonstrated a higher degree of precision in estimating temporal duration compared to the restrained group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Subjects in the free-moving group demonstrated a higher degree of precision in time estimation compared to those restricted from moving. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The behavioral result about improved precision for free-moving subjects is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The change in precision for free-moving subjects is attributed to a shift in the response threshold. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not state that the precision change is attributed specifically to a response-threshold shift, so this specific mechanistic attribution is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Movement parameters can determine eventual choice before the response is made and even before the interval has elapsed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit claim that movement parameters predict choice prior to response/interval end; that specific claim is not represented in the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Perceptual timing may be instantiated within the motor system as an ongoing readout of timing judgment and confidence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Time estimation may be instantiated within the motor system as an ongoing readout of timing judgment and confidence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: P4 and Q3 convey the same tentative conclusion about motor-system instantiation of timing, with only minor rephrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The rats’ discrimination accuracy in the task is level-invariant, consistent with Weber’s law. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The experiments revealed the existence of a psychophysical regularity termed time–intensity equivalence in discrimination (TIED). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication formalizes the observed level-invariant accuracy as the TIED regularity; this names and clarifies the original empirical observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shape of the reaction time distributions is also level-invariant, implying that the only behavioral effect of changes in the overall level of the sounds is a uniform scaling of time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Discriminative choices must be based on bounded exact accumulation of evidence. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's empirical description of RT scaling is extended in the publication into a mechanistic claim about bounded exact accumulation; this represents a substantive revision and broadening toward a proposed computational mechanism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Weber’s law breaks down if the stimulus duration is capped at values shorter than the typical reaction time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly states the conditional breakdown of Weber's law for very short stimulus durations, so this specific caveat appears omitted from the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Various manipulations of motivation were unable to alter the rats’ psychometric function, demonstrating the stability of the just-noticeable-difference. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's summarized claims do not mention the negative result about motivational manipulations, so this specific experimental observation is not reflected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results establish the mechanistic foundation of the process of intensity discrimination and clarify the factors that limit the precision of sensory systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mechanism identified is necessary for the TIED to hold and is sufficient to provide a virtually complete quantitative description of the behavior of the rats. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the mechanistic claim more explicit and stronger (stating necessity and sufficiency), thus clarifying and amplifying the original claim about establishing a mechanistic foundation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework introduced allows for end-to-end integrative modeling of 3D single-cell multi-channel fluorescent image data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework introduced allows for end-to-end integrative modeling of 3D single-cell multi-channel fluorescent image data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim about the end-to-end modeling framework.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can impute structures in cells where they were not imaged and quantify the variation in the location of all subcellular structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model can predict plausible locations of subcellular structures in cells where these structures were not imaged. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the imputation claim but rephrases it as predicting plausible locations; the quantification aspect is treated elsewhere, so this is a clarification of wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The latent representations of drugged cells differ from unperturbed cells, as expected by on-target effects of the drugs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The latent representations of drugged cells differ from unperturbed cells, indicating on-target effects of the drugs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the interpretation that the latent differences indicate on-target effects; this is a minor clarification/explicit interpretation of the same finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model is flexible and can be trained on images of arbitrary subcellular structures and at varying degrees of sparsity and reconstruction fidelity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained model quantifies the variation in the location of subcellular structures by generating plausible instantiations in arbitrary cell geometries. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the model's flexibility as an ability to generate plausible instantiations across arbitrary geometries and quantify variation; this clarifies/specifies the preprint's flexibility claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LCIdb is a curated, large-sized dataset of drug-target interactions that offers extensive coverage of both the molecule and druggable protein spaces. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LCIdb is a curated, large-sized dataset of drug-target interactions that offers extensive coverage of both the molecule and druggable protein spaces. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication statement is essentially identical in wording and scope to the preprint contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Komet, the proposed DTI prediction pipeline, is designed for scalability without compromising performance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Komet, the proposed DTI prediction pipeline, is designed for scalability without compromising performance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim about Komet's design and scalability with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Komet demonstrates superior scalability and prediction performance compared to state-of-the-art deep learning approaches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Komet displays superior scalability and prediction performance compared to state-of-the-art deep learning approaches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result claim is restated in the publication with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Komet shows strong generalization properties, as evidenced by its performance on an external dataset and the publicly available ℒℌ benchmark. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Komet demonstrates strong generalization properties, as evidenced by its performance on an external dataset and the publicly available LH benchmark. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same claim about generalization and the external/LH benchmark with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The microorganisms isolated from the ISS have adapted to life in space. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The comparative genomic analysis reveals common characteristics and specific genetic traits unique to ISS microorganisms, indicating adaptations to life in space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the adaptation conclusion with comparative genomic evidence, making the claim more specific while keeping the original conclusion and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified specific genetic traits unique to ISS microorganisms that suggest adaptations to manage hypoosmotic stress and increased DNA repair activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adaptations include mechanisms for managing hypoosmotic stress, increased DNA repair activity, and the presence of mobile genetic elements that enhance metabolism. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same mechanisms and adds mobile genetic elements, broadening the mechanistic scope; the certainty for this detailed listing is lower in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings indicate that certain genetic traits in ISS microorganisms may enhance their pathogenic capabilities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain genetic traits indicative of potential pathogenic capabilities have evolved in these microorganisms, which may explain their enhanced antibiotic resistance and pathogenicity in space conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication ties the potential pathogenic traits to observed antibiotic resistance/pathogenicity, clarifying the explanatory link while retaining the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The common genes facilitating adaptation to the ISS environment may enable bioproduction of essential biomolecules for future space missions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The common genes facilitating adaptation to the ISS environment may enable bioproduction of essential biomolecules for future space missions or serve as potential drug targets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the potential for bioproduction and additionally suggests these genes could be drug targets, broadening the possible applications without changing certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Microorganisms exposed to space conditions demonstrate enhanced antibiotic resistance and pathogenicity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain genetic traits indicative of potential pathogenic capabilities have evolved in these microorganisms, which may explain their enhanced antibiotic resistance and pathogenicity in space conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication links observed antibiotic resistance/pathogenicity to evolved genetic traits but presents this with somewhat lower certainty and framed as explanatory, tempering the confidence relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: snRNA-seq uncovered 15 distinct cell types in human visceral adipose tissue (hVAT) and 13 in subcutaneous adipose tissue (hSAT). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim reports the explicit counts of identified cell types; this specific result is not present in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deconvolution tools SCDC, MuSiC, and Scaden exhibited low performance in estimating cell-type proportions with a median |R| of 0.12 for estimated versus true correlations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not include the explicit low-performance comparison metrics for those external deconvolution tools, so this preprint result is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel method sNuConv achieved cell-type proportion estimation accuracy with a median R of 0.93 for hVAT and 0.95 for hSAT. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying sNucConv on bulk RNA-seq data resulted in cell-type proportion estimation with an accuracy of R = 0.93 for hVAT and R = 0.95 for hSAT. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same accuracy numbers for sNucConv/sNuConv, matching the preprint result directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resulting model from sNuConv was depot-specific, reflecting depot-differences in gene expression patterns. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting model from sNucConv was depot specific, reflecting differences in gene expression patterns between adipose tissue depots. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the depot-specific conclusion with essentially the same wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: sNuConv provides proof-of-concept for producing validated deconvolution algorithms for tissues un-amenable to single-cell RNA sequencing. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: sNucConv, a Scaden deep-learning-based deconvolution tool, was developed using snRNA-seq data to estimate cell-type composition in adipose tissues. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the concrete developed tool (sNucConv) for adipose tissues rather than the broader proof-of-concept claim; this narrows and clarifies the original contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep-learning approach was used to detect berries with at least 50±10% of visible contours, achieving an F1-score of 91.8%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep-learning approach was used to detect berries with an F1-score of 91.8% and segment them with a mean absolute error of 4.1% on their area. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same detection F1-score and adds segmentation accuracy (MAE); the core detection result is preserved and slightly clarified with an extra metric.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tracking algorithm developed allowed for the labeling and retrieval of the temporal identity of more than half of the segmented berries, with an accuracy of 98.1%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tracking algorithm allowed for the labeling and retrieval of the temporal identity of more than half of the segmented berries, achieving an accuracy of 98.1%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The tracking result, accuracy, and scope are restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method enables the first statistically relevant analysis of berry ripening kinetics with a time resolution lower than one day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method enables the first statistically relevant analysis of berry ripening kinetics with a time resolution lower than one day. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this core conclusion about temporal resolution and first statistically relevant analysis with equivalent certainty.
